## claude.ai Honeycomb
Honeycomb MCP Server - Observability Data Query Interface

<role>
You are an observability assistant for Honeycomb users. You investigate production behavior — performance, errors, deployment impact, alert state — by querying real telemetry through these tools. The workspace's actual state is only knowable through tool calls; do not reason from priors about what services, datasets, or alerts exist.

When the user names a dataset, span, or service, treat it as a starting point for discovery, not a license to skip it. The right span name within a dataset is rarely obvious from the dataset name alone, and aggregating the wrong attribute or the wrong span is the most common cause of misleading answers.
</role>

<workflow>
For most observability questions — including "how many", "what's slow", "what's erroring" — start with span discovery (list_spans, get_span_details). These tools return counts and attribute distributions directly; they often answer the question without a follow-up query. Reach for run_query only when you need a calculation, distribution, or comparison those tools can't express. Span tools work across all trace-aware datasets in scope without needing to know which dataset to target.

1. ORIENT
   - Call 'get_workspace_context' to learn the team, environments, and dataset counts. Use the returned environment slug in subsequent calls.

2. DISCOVER (pick the path that matches the question)
   - Span discovery — for "what's happening / what's slow / how many X / what does service X do":
     - 'list_spans' ranks span names by count with root_count to distinguish entry points from internal helpers. Counts here are often the answer to "how many X happened".
     - 'get_span_details' on a chosen span_name returns its populated attributes, value cardinality, and top observed values — including which spans actually populate a count-bearing attribute.
     Answer directly from these when you can — composing an equivalent run_query by hand is slower and more error-prone.
   - Alert / SLO state — for "what's bro… [truncated]

## claude.ai Linear
When passing string values to tools, send the content directly without escape sequences. For example, use real newlines in markdown content rather than literal backslash-n (\n) characters.

## plugin:cloudflare:cloudflare-builds
# Cloudflare Workers Builds Tool
* A Cloudflare Worker is a serverless function
* Workers Builds is a CI/CD system for building and deploying your Worker whenever you push code to GitHub/GitLab.

This server allows you to view and debug Cloudflare Workers Builds for your Workers (NOT Cloudflare Pages).

To get started, you can list your Workers (workers_list) and set an active Worker (workers_builds_set_active_worker).
You can then list the builds for your Worker (workers_builds_list_builds) and set an active build (workers_builds_set_active_build).
Once you have an active build, you can view the logs (workers_builds_get_build_logs).

## plugin:cloudflare:cloudflare-observability
# Cloudflare Workers Observability Tool
				* A cloudflare worker is a serverless function
				* Workers Observability is the tool to inspect the logs for your cloudflare Worker
				* Each log is a structured JSON payload with keys and values


				This server allows you to analyze your Cloudflare Workers logs and metrics.

## plugin:datadog:mcp
This is an MCP server for Datadog (a software observability platform). Consult Datadog docs if you're unsure about how something works in the platform. Docs for this MCP server can be found at https://docs.datadoghq.com/bits_ai/mcp_server/setup/ This server ships skill guides containing domain-specific guidance, workflows, and best practices that are not visible in tool names or schemas. Before invoking other tools, always perform skill discovery by running both paths in parallel: a direct load with `load_datadog_skill` using a general domain name (e.g. skill_name='datadog/traces'), and a call to `list_datadog_skills` with a fuzzy `query` based on topic keywords — load any clearly matching result from the listing along with the related skills it points to. The exception is a follow-up call in a domain whose skill you have already loaded or confirmed absent this session. If nothing clearly fits, do not load a skill — proceed with the tool descriptions. When the user asks to visualize, chart, or graph data — or when loading a data domain skill (e.g. datadog/traces, datadog/logs, datadog/metrics) — also load datadog/visualizations. Investigation results often benefit from charts.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- cloudflare:build-agent: Build an AI agent on Cloudflare using the Agents SDK
- cloudflare:build-mcp: Build a remote MCP server on Cloudflare using McpAgent
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- cloudflare:agents-sdk: Build AI agents on Cloudflare Workers using the Agents SDK. Load when creating stateful agents, durable workflows, real-time WebSocket apps, scheduled tasks, MCP servers, chat applications, voice agents, or browser automation. Covers Agent class, state management, callable RPC, Workflows, durable execution, queues, retries, observability, and React hooks. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare:cloudflare: Comprehensive Cloudflare platform skill covering Workers, Pages, storage (KV, D1, R2), AI (Workers AI, Vectorize, Agents SDK), feature flags (Flagship), networking (Tunnel, Spectrum), security (WAF, DDoS), and infrastructure-as-code (Terraform, Pulumi). Use for any Cloudflare development task. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare:cloudflare-email-service: Send and receive transactional emails with Cloudflare Email Service (Email Sending + Email Routing). Use when building email sending (Workers binding or REST API), email routing, Agents SDK email handling, or integrating email into any app — Workers, Node.js, Python, Go, etc. Also use for email deliverability, SPF/DKIM/DMARC, wrangler email setup, MCP email tools, or when a coding agent needs to send emails. Even for simple requests like "add email to my Worker" — this skill has critical config details.
- cloudflare:durable-objects: Create and review Cloudflare Durable Objects. Use when building stateful coordination (chat rooms, multiplayer games, booking systems), implementing RPC methods, SQLite storage, alarms, WebSockets, or reviewing DO code for best practices. Covers Workers integration, wrangler config, and testing with Vitest. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare:sandbox-sdk: Build sandboxed applications for secure code execution. Load when building AI code execution, code interpreters, CI/CD systems, interactive dev environments, or executing untrusted code. Covers Sandbox SDK lifecycle, commands, files, code interpreter, and preview URLs. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare:web-perf: Analyzes web performance using Chrome DevTools MCP. Measures Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) and supplementary metrics (FCP, TBT, Speed Index), identifies render-blocking resources, network dependency chains, layout shifts, caching issues, and accessibility gaps. Use when asked to audit, profile, debug, or optimize page load performance, Lighthouse scores, or site speed. Biases towards retrieval from current documentation over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare:workers-best-practices: Reviews and authors Cloudflare Workers code against production best practices. Load when writing new Workers, reviewing Worker code, configuring wrangler.jsonc, or checking for common Workers anti-patterns (streaming, floating promises, global state, secrets, bindings, observability). Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare:wrangler: Cloudflare Workers CLI for deploying, developing, and managing Workers, KV, R2, D1, Vectorize, Hyperdrive, Workers AI, Containers, Queues, Workflows, Pipelines, and Secrets Store. Load before running wrangler commands to ensure correct syntax and best practices. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- datadog:ddconfig: Configures or troubleshoots the Datadog MCP server `plugin:datadog:mcp`. Use when the user wants to change the Datadog domain, switch organizations, or when the server was previously configured but is not responding.
- datadog:ddsetup: First-time initialization of the Datadog MCP server `plugin:datadog:mcp`. When fulfilling requests that involve Datadog, use MCP tools from `plugin:datadog:mcp` over other methods. If MCP tools from `plugin:datadog:mcp` are not in your tool list, you MUST run this skill's setup procedure before attempting to fulfill the request. Relevant when the user wants to view or list dashboards or monitors, check alerts, view logs, query metrics, inspect APM traces, investigate SLOs or incidents, debug production issues, investigate errors, analyze performance, investigate a named service's health, errors, or dependencies, or access any Datadog data.
- datadog:ddtoolsets: Manages toolsets for the Datadog MCP server `plugin:datadog:mcp`. Use when the user wants to view, enable, or disable toolsets that control which tools are available on the MCP server.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

{"additions":401,"author":{"id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgyOTE3OA==","is_bot":false,"login":"nodo","name":"Andrea Nodari"},"baseRefName":"main","body":"## Problem

When two syncs touch the same mirror repo close together — rapid back-to-back webhooks, or a webhook overlapping the 15-min rekicker — the target ref moves between this job's *plan* and its *push*, and the target receive-pack rejects the stale update with `remote ref has changed`. This is a clean compare-and-swap miss, **not a failure**: the next delivery re-plans against the new tip and applies (usually a no-op, since the job that won the race already mirrored the same source state).

Until now these were classified transient → `nak`, logged at **ERROR** (`"Sync failed"`), and counted as `outcome:nak`. So they fed the `[mirror-pipeline] Mirror sync failure rate` composite's `nak+term` numerator and could page on a busy repo — despite always self-healing on redelivery. Datadog over the last 7d: **~304** spurious ERROR `Sync failed` logs / `nak` ticks in prod from this one cause.

## Change

- **`isConcurrentRefError`** (`syncer_errors.go`) — matches `remote ref has changed` (the entire-server target's receive-pack rejection) plus the git push-protocol markers `fetch first` / `non-fast-forward` / `stale info`. `does not match` is deliberately excluded as too broad.
- **`dispositionRaced` + `outcome:raced`** (`consumer.go`, `metrics.go`) — `classifyDisposition` routes concurrent-ref errors here, checked *after* `isPermanentError` so a 4xx that happens to echo one of these phrases still terminates. `disposeResult` treats raced exactly like `nak` (`NakWithDelay` → redeliver → self-heal) but logs at **WARN** and records `outcome:raced` instead of `outcome:nak`.
- **Monitor: no query change needed.** The failure-rate numerator names only `nak+term`, so `raced` drops out of the alert automatically while staying in the unfiltered denominator (correctly diluting the rate). A guardrail comment explaining this lives in `infra` (separate repo, comment-only → no terraform diff).
- **Safety net preserved:** a repo that races *every* delivery to `MaxDeliver` exhaustion still escalates to `term` (records `max_deliveries`), so a genuinely stuck repo is not hidden.

## Tests

- `TestIsConcurrentRefError` — the observed prod string + each marker, wrapping, and the excluded/negative cases.
- `TestClassifyDisposition` — new `raced` cases incl. `permanent beats raced` (ordering).
- `TestDisposeResult_Raced_NaksAndCountsRacedNotNak` — non-final → Nak, `outcome:raced`, no nak/term, no index write.
- `TestDisposeResult_Raced_LastDelivery_EscalatesToTerm` — final delivery → Term + `max_deliveries` + `outcome:term`.

`go test ./cmd/worker/`, `go vet`, `gofmt`, and `golangci-lint` all clean.

## Note (out of scope)

The larger driver of the same alert is `sts/token` errors (~2,420/7d on the same log line) — untouched here, flagged for a separate follow-up.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> **Medium Risk**
> Changes JetStream disposition, metrics, and tracing for a common retry path; misclassification could hide real failures or leave alerts noisy, though permanent errors still win and max-delivery escalation is unchanged.
> 
> **Overview**
> Concurrent target-ref compare-and-swap misses (`gitsync.ErrTargetRefMoved`) are no longer treated as transient sync failures. The worker adds **`isConcurrentRefError`** / **`shouldMarkSpanError`**, routes them through **`dispositionRaced`** and **`outcome:raced`**, and still **Nak**s for self-heal while logging **WARN** instead of ERROR and **excluding** them from the sync-failure alert’s **nak+term** numerator (and from benign APM span errors). Races that exhaust **MaxDeliver** still **Term** with **`max_deliveries`** like other transient exhaustion paths; traces get **`entire.sync.raced`** for queryability.
> 
> **git-sync** is bumped (vendored) so push rejections expose **`ErrTargetRefMoved`** / **`*RefRejectedError`** instead of relying on worker-side message substring matching. Tests cover disposition, metrics, span marking, and disposeResult raced vs final-delivery escalation.
> 
> <sup>Reviewed by [Cursor Bugbot](https://cursor.com/bugbot) for commit 3ebf937c4d5e2f57fa2d6ba786e0dcb432fb574a. Configure [here](https://www.cursor.com/dashboard/bugbot).</sup>
<!-- /CURSOR_SUMMARY -->","changedFiles":12,"comments":[{"id":"IC_kwDOSJgDfc8AAAABF7g_JQ","author":{"login":"nodo"},"authorAssociation":"MEMBER","body":"## Review summary

Did a full pass over the diff in context (`consumer.go`, `syncer_errors.go`, `metrics.go` + tests). No code changes pushed — I found nothing to fix, and didn't want to add churn to a clean PR.

**Reviewer comments addressed:** none open — no review threads or comments existed at review time, and `test` + Cursor Bugbot are both green.

**My own review (no issues found):**
- **Classification ordering** — `isConcurrentRefError` runs *after* `isPermanentError`, so a 4xx that echoes `non-fast-forward` etc. still terms rather than masquerading as a benign race. Correct and covered by the `permanent beats raced` case.
- **Disposition** — `dispositionRaced` matches the `nak` path exactly: `NakWithDelay` → redeliver (re-plan against the new tip → self-heal, usually a no-op), and Term + `max_deliveries` at `consumerMaxDeliver` exhaustion. Redeliver-and-re-plan is correct in every concurrent-ref scenario (winner mirrored the same source state → no-op; source advanced → applies newer state). Bounded by the existing max-deliver backstop, so no new unbounded-retry risk.
- **Metrics** — `raced` stays out of the `nak+term` numerator while remaining in the denominator (correctly dilutes the rate), and `last_success_unix` is left untouched (only `outcomeAck` stamps it). Counter description updated consistently.
- **Consistency** — no in-repo dashboard/monitor computes the failure rate as `total − ack`; the only failure-rate query lives in the separate infra repo (per the PR description), so nothing else in this repo needs to learn about `raced`.

**Intentionally left alone:** the `fetch first` / `stale info` / `non-fast-forward` hints are fairly generic substrings that could in principle false-positive on an unrelated message. I left them as-is because a false positive is benign and bounded — it produces the *same* redelivery as a nak and still terms at exhaustion; only the observability label changes — and they're documented as forward-looking coverage for a `--force-with-lease` path that isn't active today. The `sts/token` driver is correctly flagged out of scope.

**Test results (this environment, go 1.26.2):**
- `go test ./cmd/worker/` → ok
- `go vet ./cmd/worker/` → clean
- `gofmt -l cmd/worker/*.go` → clean

LGTM.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>","messageHeadline":"worker: classify concurrent-ref pushes as raced, not sync failures","oid":"a33c10c0e4af46c6e440a94191464e9f2d27a300"},{"authoredDate":"2026-06-13T01:28:36Z","authors":[{"email":"andrea@entire.io","id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgyOTE3OA==","login":"nodo","name":"Andrea Nodari"},{"email":"noreply@anthropic.com","id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgxODQ3","login":"claude","name":"Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context)"}],"committedDate":"2026-06-13T01:28:36Z","messageBody":"…(#137)

* worker: classify concurrent-ref races via git-sync ErrTargetRefMoved

git-sync now exposes a typed ErrTargetRefMoved sentinel for concurrent
target-ref rejections (entireio/git-sync#71). Switch the worker's
isConcurrentRefError from substring-matching the free-form receive-pack
message to errors.Is(err, gitsync.ErrTargetRefMoved), so the benign-race
classification is owned by the library — which has the protocol context —
instead of being guessed from a flattened string.

Keep a thin residual substring check for "non-fast-forward" / "fetch first"
on top of the typed check: git-sync deliberately leaves those ambiguous
markers out of the sentinel because it can't assume the caller never
force-pushes, but this worker replicates with no force flags and plans
against session-start tips, so for it those markers do mean a race. In
practice entire-server emits "remote ref has changed" (caught by the
sentinel); the residual is defence-in-depth for other target phrasings.

Bumps entire.io/entire/git-sync to the merged main commit and re-vendors.
Repin to v0.7.0 once git-sync cuts the release.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

* worker: drop residual marker matching, rely solely on ErrTargetRefMoved

Remove the worker-side substring check for "non-fast-forward" / "fetch first".
Against entire-server targets a concurrent move always surfaces as the
compare-and-swap reason "remote ref has changed" (wrapped as
gitsync.ErrTargetRefMoved), so the typed sentinel already covers every real
race — confirmed by telemetry (100% of observed raced events were that
string). A bare "non-fast-forward" here would mean the source rewound and
needs a force the worker doesn't do — a genuine failure, not a race — so
matching it as raced was at best dead code and at worst a misclassification.

This also restores the point of the typed error: classification lives in
git-sync, not in worker substring guesses. If a non-entire target is ever
added whose receive-pack phrases CAS misses differently, broaden git-sync's
marker set rather than re-adding matching here.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

---------

Co-authored-by: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>","messageHeadline":"worker: classify concurrent-ref races via git-sync ErrTargetRefMoved …","oid":"76fa443030ec94d9fa8ea28520befbaf6bb8d0fd"},{"authoredDate":"2026-06-13T01:41:05Z","authors":[{"email":"andrea@entire.io","id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgyOTE3OA==","login":"nodo","name":"Andrea Nodari"},{"email":"noreply@anthropic.com","id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgxODQ3","login":"claude","name":"Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context)"}],"committedDate":"2026-06-13T01:41:05Z","messageBody":"dispositionRaced already excludes benign concurrent-ref races from the
sync-failure metric, but handleGitJob still marked the mirror.consume span as
an error before disposeResult classified them — so races kept inflating the
mirror-worker APM Consumer error rate (trace.Consumer.errors) on the
dashboard, even though the metric alert was clean.

Extract shouldMarkSpanError (pure, unit-tested) and skip RecordError /
SetStatus(Error) for a redelivering race, matching the metric's exclusion. A
race that has exhausted redelivery escalates to term and stays marked, so a

{"additions":401,"author":{"id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgyOTE3OA==","is_bot":false,"login":"nodo","name":"Andrea Nodari"},"baseRefName":"main","body":"## Problem

When two syncs touch the same mirror repo close together — rapid back-to-back webhooks, or a webhook overlapping the 15-min rekicker — the target ref moves between this job's *plan* and its *push*, and the target receive-pack rejects the stale update with `remote ref has changed`. This is a clean compare-and-swap miss, **not a failure**: the next delivery re-plans against the new tip and applies (usually a no-op, since the job that won the race already mirrored the same source state).

Until now these were classified transient → `nak`, logged at **ERROR** (`"Sync failed"`), and counted as `outcome:nak`. So they fed the `[mirror-pipeline] Mirror sync failure rate` composite's `nak+term` numerator and could page on a busy repo — despite always self-healing on redelivery. Datadog over the last 7d: **~304** spurious ERROR `Sync failed` logs / `nak` ticks in prod from this one cause.

## Change

- **`isConcurrentRefError`** (`syncer_errors.go`) — matches `remote ref has changed` (the entire-server target's receive-pack rejection) plus the git push-protocol markers `fetch first` / `non-fast-forward` / `stale info`. `does not match` is deliberately excluded as too broad.
- **`dispositionRaced` + `outcome:raced`** (`consumer.go`, `metrics.go`) — `classifyDisposition` routes concurrent-ref errors here, checked *after* `isPermanentError` so a 4xx that happens to echo one of these phrases still terminates. `disposeResult` treats raced exactly like `nak` (`NakWithDelay` → redeliver → self-heal) but logs at **WARN** and records `outcome:raced` instead of `outcome:nak`.
- **Monitor: no query change needed.** The failure-rate numerator names only `nak+term`, so `raced` drops out of the alert automatically while staying in the unfiltered denominator (correctly diluting the rate). A guardrail comment explaining this lives in `infra` (separate repo, comment-only → no terraform diff).
- **Safety net preserved:** a repo that races *every* delivery to `MaxDeliver` exhaustion still escalates to `term` (records `max_deliveries`), so a genuinely stuck repo is not hidden.

## Tests

- `TestIsConcurrentRefError` — the observed prod string + each marker, wrapping, and the excluded/negative cases.
- `TestClassifyDisposition` — new `raced` cases incl. `permanent beats raced` (ordering).
- `TestDisposeResult_Raced_NaksAndCountsRacedNotNak` — non-final → Nak, `outcome:raced`, no nak/term, no index write.
- `TestDisposeResult_Raced_LastDelivery_EscalatesToTerm` — final delivery → Term + `max_deliveries` + `outcome:term`.

`go test ./cmd/worker/`, `go vet`, `gofmt`, and `golangci-lint` all clean.

## Note (out of scope)

The larger driver of the same alert is `sts/token` errors (~2,420/7d on the same log line) — untouched here, flagged for a separate follow-up.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

> [!NOTE]
> **Medium Risk**
> Changes JetStream disposition, metrics, and tracing for a common retry path; misclassification could hide real failures or leave alerts noisy, though permanent errors still win and max-delivery escalation is unchanged.
> 
> **Overview**
> Concurrent target-ref compare-and-swap misses (`gitsync.ErrTargetRefMoved`) are no longer treated as transient sync failures. The worker adds **`isConcurrentRefError`** / **`shouldMarkSpanError`**, routes them through **`dispositionRaced`** and **`outcome:raced`**, and still **Nak**s for self-heal while logging **WARN** instead of ERROR and **excluding** them from the sync-failure alert’s **nak+term** numerator (and from benign APM span errors). Races that exhaust **MaxDeliver** still **Term** with **`max_deliveries`** like other transient exhaustion paths; traces get **`entire.sync.raced`** for queryability.
> 
> **git-sync** is bumped (vendored) so push rejections expose **`ErrTargetRefMoved`** / **`*RefRejectedError`** instead of relying on worker-side message substring matching. Tests cover disposition, metrics, span marking, and disposeResult raced vs final-delivery escalation.
> 
> <sup>Reviewed by [Cursor Bugbot](https://cursor.com/bugbot) for commit 3ebf937c4d5e2f57fa2d6ba786e0dcb432fb574a. Configure [here](https://www.cursor.com/dashboard/bugbot).</sup>
<!-- /CURSOR_SUMMARY -->","changedFiles":12,"comments":[{"id":"IC_kwDOSJgDfc8AAAABF7g_JQ","author":{"login":"nodo"},"authorAssociation":"MEMBER","body":"## Review summary

Did a full pass over the diff in context (`consumer.go`, `syncer_errors.go`, `metrics.go` + tests). No code changes pushed — I found nothing to fix, and didn't want to add churn to a clean PR.

**Reviewer comments addressed:** none open — no review threads or comments existed at review time, and `test` + Cursor Bugbot are both green.

**My own review (no issues found):**
- **Classification ordering** — `isConcurrentRefError` runs *after* `isPermanentError`, so a 4xx that echoes `non-fast-forward` etc. still terms rather than masquerading as a benign race. Correct and covered by the `permanent beats raced` case.
- **Disposition** — `dispositionRaced` matches the `nak` path exactly: `NakWithDelay` → redeliver (re-plan against the new tip → self-heal, usually a no-op), and Term + `max_deliveries` at `consumerMaxDeliver` exhaustion. Redeliver-and-re-plan is correct in every concurrent-ref scenario (winner mirrored the same source state → no-op; source advanced → applies newer state). Bounded by the existing max-deliver backstop, so no new unbounded-retry risk.
- **Metrics** — `raced` stays out of the `nak+term` numerator while remaining in the denominator (correctly dilutes the rate), and `last_success_unix` is left untouched (only `outcomeAck` stamps it). Counter description updated consistently.
- **Consistency** — no in-repo dashboard/monitor computes the failure rate as `total − ack`; the only failure-rate query lives in the separate infra repo (per the PR description), so nothing else in this repo needs to learn about `raced`.

**Intentionally left alone:** the `fetch first` / `stale info` / `non-fast-forward` hints are fairly generic substrings that could in principle false-positive on an unrelated message. I left them as-is because a false positive is benign and bounded — it produces the *same* redelivery as a nak and still terms at exhaustion; only the observability label changes — and they're documented as forward-looking coverage for a `--force-with-lease` path that isn't active today. The `sts/token` driver is correctly flagged out of scope.

**Test results (this environment, go 1.26.2):**
- `go test ./cmd/worker/` → ok
- `go vet ./cmd/worker/` → clean
- `gofmt -l cmd/worker/*.go` → clean

LGTM.

* worker: classify concurrent-ref races via git-sync ErrTargetRefMoved

git-sync now exposes a typed ErrTargetRefMoved sentinel for concurrent
target-ref rejections (entireio/git-sync#71). Switch the worker's
isConcurrentRefError from substring-matching the free-form receive-pack
message to errors.Is(err, gitsync.ErrTargetRefMoved), so the benign-race
classification is owned by the library — which has the protocol context —
instead of being guessed from a flattened string.

Keep a thin residual substring check for "non-fast-forward" / "fetch first"
on top of the typed check: git-sync deliberately leaves those ambiguous
markers out of the sentinel because it can't assume the caller never
force-pushes, but this worker replicates with no force flags and plans
against session-start tips, so for it those markers do mean a race. In
practice entire-server emits "remote ref has changed" (caught by the
sentinel); the residual is defence-in-depth for other target phrasings.

Bumps entire.io/entire/git-sync to the merged main commit and re-vendors.
Repin to v0.7.0 once git-sync cuts the release.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

* worker: drop residual marker matching, rely solely on ErrTargetRefMoved

Remove the worker-side substring check for "non-fast-forward" / "fetch first".
Against entire-server targets a concurrent move always surfaces as the
compare-and-swap reason "remote ref has changed" (wrapped as
gitsync.ErrTargetRefMoved), so the typed sentinel already covers every real
race — confirmed by telemetry (100% of observed raced events were that
string). A bare "non-fast-forward" here would mean the source rewound and
needs a force the worker doesn't do — a genuine failure, not a race — so
matching it as raced was at best dead code and at worst a misclassification.

This also restores the point of the typed error: classification lives in
git-sync, not in worker substring guesses. If a non-entire target is ever
added whose receive-pack phrases CAS misses differently, broaden git-sync's
marker set rather than re-adding matching here.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

---------

diff --git a/cmd/worker/consumer.go b/cmd/worker/consumer.go
index 35a0ab6..9999492 100644
--- a/cmd/worker/consumer.go
+++ b/cmd/worker/consumer.go
@@ -294,9 +294,17 @@ func (c *consumer) handleGitJob(ctx context.Context, span trace.Span, msg *nats.
 		err = transientErr(fmt.Errorf("sync exceeded the %v budget: %w", c.syncTimeout, err))
 	}
 
-	// errSourceNoRefs is a benign no-op (empty source), not a failure — leave
-	// the span unmarked so it doesn't surface as an error in traces.
-	if err != nil && !errors.Is(err, errSourceNoRefs) {
+	// Tag concurrent-ref races so they stay queryable in traces even though
+	// (below) they're deliberately not marked as span errors.
+	if isConcurrentRefError(err) {
+		span.SetAttributes(attribute.Bool("entire.sync.raced", true))
+	}
+	// Mark the span as an error only for genuine failures. Benign cases —
+	// errSourceNoRefs (empty source, acked as a no-op) and a redelivering
+	// concurrent-ref race — are excluded so they don't inflate the
+	// mirror.consume APM error rate, matching their exclusion from the outcome
+	// metric. See shouldMarkSpanError.
+	if shouldMarkSpanError(err, isLastDelivery(msg)) {
 		span.RecordError(err)
 		span.SetStatus(otelcodes.Error, "sync failed")
 		span.SetAttributes(attribute.Bool("entire.error.permanent", isPermanentError(err)))
@@ -351,6 +359,30 @@ func (c *consumer) disposeResult(ctx context.Context, m ackableMsg, err error, p
 		slog.ErrorContext(ctx, "Sync failed", slog.Any("error", err))
 		c.metrics.recordSyncOutcome(ctx, provider, c.jurisdiction, outcomeNak)
 		_ = m.NakWithDelay(consumerFailureDelay)
+	case dispositionRaced:
+		// Target ref moved between this job's plan and its push — a second sync
+		// of the same repo won the race (rapid webhooks, or a webhook
+		// overlapping the rekicker). Redeliver like a nak so the next delivery
+		// re-plans against the new tip and applies cleanly (usually a no-op,
+		// since the winner already mirrored the same source state), but record
+		// outcomeRaced + WARN so it stays out of the sync-failure alert's
+		// nak+term numerator: it self-heals and pages no one.
+		//
+		// Final-delivery escalation is identical to the nak path: if a repo
+		// somehow races every delivery to exhaustion the contention is no longer
+		// benign, so we record terminal (max_deliveries) and label outcome=term
+		// — that DOES reach the alert, which is what we want for a genuinely
+		// stuck repo. Fail open on the index write exactly like dispositionNak.
+		if isLastDelivery(m) {
+			c.recordTerminal(ctx, ulid, subject, payload, seq, failindex.ReasonMaxDeliveries, failindex.SourceWorkerExhausted, err)
+			slog.ErrorContext(ctx, "Sync kept racing on target ref; deliveries exhausted, terminating", slog.Any("error", err))
+			c.metrics.recordSyncOutcome(ctx, provider, c.jurisdiction, outcomeTerm)
+			_ = m.Term()
+			return
+		}
---DIFFSTAT---

cmd/worker/metrics.go

+func TestDisposeResult_Raced_NaksAndCountsRacedNotNak(t *testing.T) {
+	// A concurrent-ref miss ("remote ref has changed") on a non-final delivery
+	// must redeliver (Nak) so it self-heals on the next attempt, but be counted
+	// as outcome:raced — never nak/term — so it stays out of the sync-failure
+	// alert's nak+term numerator. No index write on a non-final delivery.
+	m, r := newTestMetrics(t)
+	idx := &fakeIndex{}
+	c := newConsumer(m, idx)
+	msg := msgAt(4, 1) // early delivery, not exhausted
+	err := transientErr(fmt.Errorf("replicate relay failed: replicate execute: %w", gitsync.ErrTargetRefMoved))
+
+	c.disposeResult(context.Background(), msg, err, "github", tULID, tSubj, tPayload, false)
+
+	if !msg.naked || msg.termed || msg.acked {
+		t.Fatalf("raced non-final must Nak (redeliver): acked=%v termed=%v naked=%v", msg.acked, msg.termed, msg.naked)
+	}
+	if len(idx.records) != 0 {
+		t.Fatalf("non-final raced must not write the failure index, got %+v", idx.records)
+	}
+	if got := counterValue(t, r, "mirror.sync.completed", "outcome", "raced"); got != 1 {
+		t.Fatalf("outcome=raced count = %d, want 1", got)
+	}
+	if got := counterValue(t, r, "mirror.sync.completed", "outcome", "nak"); got != 0 {
+		t.Fatalf("must NOT count nak, got %d", got)
+	}
+	if got := counterValue(t, r, "mirror.sync.completed", "outcome", "term"); got != 0 {
+		t.Fatalf("must NOT count term, got %d", got)
+	}
+}
+
+func TestDisposeResult_Raced_LastDelivery_EscalatesToTerm(t *testing.T) {
+	// A repo that races every delivery to MaxDeliver exhaustion is no longer
+	// benign: the final delivery records terminal (max_deliveries) and labels
+	// outcome=term, so a genuinely stuck repo still reaches the alert rather
+	// than self-healing forever under outcome:raced.
+	m, r := newTestMetrics(t)
+	idx := &fakeIndex{}
+	c := newConsumer(m, idx)
+	msg := msgAt(5, consumerMaxDeliver) // final delivery
+	err := transientErr(fmt.Errorf("report-status: command error on refs/heads/main: %w", gitsync.ErrTargetRefMoved))
+
+	c.disposeResult(context.Background(), msg, err, "github", tULID, tSubj, tPayload, false)
+
+	if !msg.termed || msg.naked || msg.acked {
+		t.Fatalf("raced final delivery must Term: acked=%v termed=%v naked=%v", msg.acked, msg.termed, msg.naked)
+	}
+	if len(idx.records) != 1 || idx.records[0].fs.Reason != failindex.ReasonMaxDeliveries {
+		t.Fatalf("want one max_deliveries record, got %+v", idx.records)
+	}
+	if got := counterValue(t, r, "mirror.sync.completed", "outcome", "term"); got != 1 {
+		t.Fatalf("outcome=term count = %d, want 1", got)
+	}
+	if got := counterValue(t, r, "mirror.sync.completed", "outcome", "raced"); got != 0 {
+		t.Fatalf("must NOT count raced on exhaustion, got %d", got)
+	}
+}

+// isConcurrentRefError reports whether err is a benign "target ref moved
+// concurrently" rejection — almost always a second sync of the same repo landing
+// first (rapid back-to-back webhooks, or a webhook overlapping the 15-min
+// rekicker). It is a clean compare-and-swap miss, not a real failure: the next
+// delivery re-plans against the new tip and applies (it usually no-ops, because
+// the job that won the race already mirrored the same source state). The
+// disposition layer (classifyDisposition) routes these to dispositionRaced so
+// they redeliver like a nak but stay out of the sync-failure alert's nak+term
+// numerator.
+//
+// We rely entirely on the typed gitsync.ErrTargetRefMoved sentinel; git-sync
+// owns the classification. Against entire-server targets a race always surfaces
+// as its compare-and-swap reason ("remote ref has changed"), which git-sync
+// wraps as this sentinel — so no worker-side substring matching is needed, and
+// none is done (a "non-fast-forward" rejection here would mean the source
+// rewound and needs a force the worker doesn't do, NOT a race). If a non-entire
+// target is ever introduced whose receive-pack phrases CAS misses differently,
+// broaden git-sync's marker set rather than re-adding matching here.
+//
+// These are a strict subset of the transient class (classifySyncError never
+// marks them permanent), so isPermanentError takes precedence in
+// classifyDisposition.
+func isConcurrentRefError(err error) bool {
+	return errors.Is(err, gitsync.ErrTargetRefMoved)
+}
+
+// shouldMarkSpanError reports whether a sync error should surface as an error on
+// the mirror.consume APM span. It mirrors the disposition layer's notion of
+// "failure" so the trace error rate doesn't disagree with the outcome metric.
+// Two benign cases are excluded: an empty source (errSourceNoRefs, acked as a
+// no-op) and a concurrent-ref race that will redeliver and self-heal
+// (dispositionRaced). A race that has EXHAUSTED redelivery (lastDelivery) is no
+// longer benign — disposeResult escalates it to term — so it stays marked.
+// Pure (no span/IO) so the decision is unit-testable; the handler owns the
+// span side effects.
+func shouldMarkSpanError(err error, lastDelivery bool) bool {
+	if err == nil {
+		return false
+	}
+	if errors.Is(err, errSourceNoRefs) {
+		return false
+	}
+	if isConcurrentRefError(err) && !lastDelivery {
+		return false
+	}
+	return true
+}

+func TestDisposeResult_Raced_NaksAndCountsRacedNotNak(t *testing.T) {
+	// A concurrent-ref miss ("remote ref has changed") on a non-final delivery
+	// must redeliver (Nak) so it self-heals on the next attempt, but be counted
+	// as outcome:raced — never nak/term — so it stays out of the sync-failure
+	// alert's nak+term numerator. No index write on a non-final delivery.
+	m, r := newTestMetrics(t)
+	idx := &fakeIndex{}
+	c := newConsumer(m, idx)
+	msg := msgAt(4, 1) // early delivery, not exhausted
+	err := transientErr(fmt.Errorf("replicate relay failed: replicate execute: %w", gitsync.ErrTargetRefMoved))
+
+	c.disposeResult(context.Background(), msg, err, "github", tULID, tSubj, tPayload, false)
+
+	if !msg.naked || msg.termed || msg.acked {
+		t.Fatalf("raced non-final must Nak (redeliver): acked=%v termed=%v naked=%v", msg.acked, msg.termed, msg.naked)
+	}
+	if len(idx.records) != 0 {
+		t.Fatalf("non-final raced must not write the failure index, got %+v", idx.records)
+	}
+	if got := counterValue(t, r, "mirror.sync.completed", "outcome", "raced"); got != 1 {
+		t.Fatalf("outcome=raced count = %d, want 1", got)
+	}
+	if got := counterValue(t, r, "mirror.sync.completed", "outcome", "nak"); got != 0 {
+		t.Fatalf("must NOT count nak, got %d", got)
+	}
+	if got := counterValue(t, r, "mirror.sync.completed", "outcome", "term"); got != 0 {
+		t.Fatalf("must NOT count term, got %d", got)
+	}
+}
+
+func TestDisposeResult_Raced_LastDelivery_EscalatesToTerm(t *testing.T) {
+	// A repo that races every delivery to MaxDeliver exhaustion is no longer
+	// benign: the final delivery records terminal (max_deliveries) and labels
+	// outcome=term, so a genuinely stuck repo still reaches the alert rather
+	// than self-healing forever under outcome:raced.
+	m, r := newTestMetrics(t)
+	idx := &fakeIndex{}
+	c := newConsumer(m, idx)
+	msg := msgAt(5, consumerMaxDeliver) // final delivery
+	err := transientErr(fmt.Errorf("report-status: command error on refs/heads/main: %w", gitsync.ErrTargetRefMoved))
+
+	c.disposeResult(context.Background(), msg, err, "github", tULID, tSubj, tPayload, false)
+
+	if !msg.termed || msg.naked || msg.acked {
+		t.Fatalf("raced final delivery must Term: acked=%v termed=%v naked=%v", msg.acked, msg.termed, msg.naked)
+	}
+	if len(idx.records) != 1 || idx.records[0].fs.Reason != failindex.ReasonMaxDeliveries {
+		t.Fatalf("want one max_deliveries record, got %+v", idx.records)
+	}
+	if got := counterValue(t, r, "mirror.sync.completed", "outcome", "term"); got != 1 {
+		t.Fatalf("outcome=term count = %d, want 1", got)
+	}
+	if got := counterValue(t, r, "mirror.sync.completed", "outcome", "raced"); got != 0 {
+		t.Fatalf("must NOT count raced on exhaustion, got %d", got)
+	}
+}

+// isConcurrentRefError reports whether err is a benign "target ref moved
+// concurrently" rejection — almost always a second sync of the same repo landing
+// first (rapid back-to-back webhooks, or a webhook overlapping the 15-min
+// rekicker). It is a clean compare-and-swap miss, not a real failure: the next
+// delivery re-plans against the new tip and applies (it usually no-ops, because
+// the job that won the race already mirrored the same source state). The
+// disposition layer (classifyDisposition) routes these to dispositionRaced so
+// they redeliver like a nak but stay out of the sync-failure alert's nak+term
+// numerator.
+//
+// We rely entirely on the typed gitsync.ErrTargetRefMoved sentinel; git-sync
+// owns the classification. Against entire-server targets a race always surfaces
+// as its compare-and-swap reason ("remote ref has changed"), which git-sync
+// wraps as this sentinel — so no worker-side substring matching is needed, and
+// none is done (a "non-fast-forward" rejection here would mean the source
+// rewound and needs a force the worker doesn't do, NOT a race). If a non-entire
+// target is ever introduced whose receive-pack phrases CAS misses differently,
+// broaden git-sync's marker set rather than re-adding matching here.
+//
+// These are a strict subset of the transient class (classifySyncError never
+// marks them permanent), so isPermanentError takes precedence in
+// classifyDisposition.
+func isConcurrentRefError(err error) bool {
+	return errors.Is(err, gitsync.ErrTargetRefMoved)
+}
+
+// shouldMarkSpanError reports whether a sync error should surface as an error on
+// the mirror.consume APM span. It mirrors the disposition layer's notion of
+// "failure" so the trace error rate doesn't disagree with the outcome metric.
+// Two benign cases are excluded: an empty source (errSourceNoRefs, acked as a
+// no-op) and a concurrent-ref race that will redeliver and self-heal
+// (dispositionRaced). A race that has EXHAUSTED redelivery (lastDelivery) is no
+// longer benign — disposeResult escalates it to term — so it stays marked.
+// Pure (no span/IO) so the decision is unit-testable; the handler owns the
+// span side effects.
+func shouldMarkSpanError(err error, lastDelivery bool) bool {
+	if err == nil {
+		return false
+	}
+	if errors.Is(err, errSourceNoRefs) {
+		return false
+	}
+	if isConcurrentRefError(err) && !lastDelivery {
+		return false
+	}
+	return true
+}

    25		Name   plumbing.ReferenceName
    26		Old    plumbing.Hash
    27		New    plumbing.Hash
    28		Delete bool
    29	}
    30	
    31	// Pusher wraps target-side receive-pack state behind a smaller execution API.
    32	// When OnRejection is non-nil, per-ref ng statuses invoke it instead of erroring;
    33	// pack-level unpack failure remains fatal.
    34	//
    35	// Returned by NewPusher as a pointer so callers can attach OnRejection after
    36	// construction without worrying about whether downstream strategies have
    37	// already captured a value copy.
    38	type Pusher struct {
    39		Conn        Conn
    40		Adv         *packp.AdvRefs
    41		Verbose     bool
    42		OnRejection func(refName plumbing.ReferenceName, status string)
    43	}
    44	
    45	// NewPusher builds a target-side push executor.
    46	func NewPusher(conn Conn, adv *packp.AdvRefs, verbose bool) *Pusher {
    47		return &Pusher{Conn: conn, Adv: adv, Verbose: verbose}
    48	}
    49	
    50	// PushPack streams a pack to the target.
    51	func (p *Pusher) PushPack(ctx context.Context, commands []PushCommand, pack io.ReadCloser) error {
    52		return PushPack(ctx, p.Conn, p.Adv, commands, pack, p.Verbose, p.OnRejection)
    53	}
    54	
    55	// PushCommands sends ref-only updates without a pack.
    56	func (p *Pusher) PushCommands(ctx context.Context, commands []PushCommand) error {
    57		return PushCommands(ctx, p.Conn, p.Adv, commands, p.Verbose, p.OnRejection)
    58	}
    59	
    60	// PushObjects encodes and pushes locally materialized objects.
    61	func (p *Pusher) PushObjects(ctx context.Context, commands []PushCommand, store storer.Storer, hashes []plumbing.Hash) error {
    62		return PushObjects(ctx, p.Conn, p.Adv, commands, store, hashes, p.Verbose, p.OnRejection)
    63	}
    64	
    65	// buildUpdateRequest builds the receive-pack update request.
    66	func buildUpdateRequest(
    67		adv *packp.AdvRefs,
    68		commands []PushCommand,
    69		verbose bool,
    70	) (*packp.UpdateRequests, bool, bool, error) {
    71		req := &packp.UpdateRequests{}
    72		if sb := PreferredSideband(&adv.Capabilities); sb != "" {
    73			req.Capabilities.Set(sb)
    74		}
    75		if adv.Capabilities.Supports(capability.ReportStatus) {
    76			req.Capabilities.Set(capability.ReportStatus)
    77		}
    78	
    79		hasDelete := false
    80		hasUpdates := false
    81		for _, cmd := range commands {
    82			c := &packp.Command{Name: cmd.Name, Old: cmd.Old}
    83			if cmd.Delete {
    84				c.New = plumbing.ZeroHash
    85				hasDelete = true
    86			} else {
    87				c.New = cmd.New
    88				hasUpdates = true
    89			}
    90			req.Commands = append(req.Commands, c)
    91		}
    92	
    93		if hasDelete {
    94			if !adv.Capabilities.Supports(capability.DeleteRefs) {
    95				return nil, false, false, errors.New("target does not support delete-refs")
    96			}
    97			req.Capabilities.Set(capability.DeleteRefs)
    98		}
    99	
   100		_ = verbose // progress handling is server-side in HTTP mode
   101		return req, hasDelete, hasUpdates, nil
   102	}
   103	
   104	// leaseFailureMarkers are receive-pack ng reason substrings that indicate the
   105	// captured target tip didn't match what was on the server at push time. Match
   106	// is case-insensitive. CommandStatusErr.Status is a free-form string in go-git,
   107	// so substring matching is the only option absent upstream sentinels.
   108	var leaseFailureMarkers = []string{
   109		"stale info",
   110		"fetch first",
   111		"non-fast-forward",
   112		"does not match",
   113	}
   114	
   115	// IsLeaseFailure reports whether a receive-pack ng reason indicates the
   116	// captured target tip no longer matched at push time. Callers that downgrade
   117	// per-ref rejections to warnings (BestEffort) must still treat these as fatal
   118	// to preserve --force-with-lease semantics.
   119	func IsLeaseFailure(status string) bool {
   120		lowered := strings.ToLower(status)
   121		for _, marker := range leaseFailureMarkers {
   122			if strings.Contains(lowered, marker) {
   123				return true
   124			}
   125		}
   126		return false
   127	}
   128	
   129	// annotateLeaseFailure wraps a lease-failure CommandStatusErr with a retry/
   130	// override hint. Other receive-pack errors pass through unchanged.
   131	func annotateLeaseFailure(err error) error {
   132		var cs *packp.CommandStatusErr
   133		if !errors.As(err, &cs) {
   134			return err
   135		}
   136		if !IsLeaseFailure(cs.Status) {
   137			return err
   138		}
   139		return fmt.Errorf("%w (target ref %s moved or differs from session start; rerun, or use --force-blind to overwrite)", err, cs.ReferenceName)
   140	}
   141	
   142	// ErrTargetRefMoved is reported (wrapped) when a push to the target was rejected
   143	// because the target ref changed concurrently between this run's plan and its
   144	// push — a benign, retryable compare-and-swap / lease miss rather than a real
   145	// failure. Test for it with errors.Is(err, ErrTargetRefMoved); the concrete
   146	// error in the chain is a *RefRejectedError. Re-exported publicly as
   147	// gitsync.ErrTargetRefMoved.
   148	var ErrTargetRefMoved = errors.New("target ref moved concurrently")
   149	
   150	// RefRejectedError is a single per-ref "ng" status returned by the target's
   151	// receive-pack report-status. Ref is the rejected ref; Reason is the raw,
   152	// server-defined reason text — the git wire protocol carries no structured error
   153	// code, so Reason is free-form and server-specific. Reach it with errors.As.
   154	// Rejections git-sync can prove are concurrent target-ref moves additionally
   155	// satisfy errors.Is(err, ErrTargetRefMoved), letting callers branch on the cause
   156	// without substring-matching Reason themselves. Re-exported publicly as
   157	// gitsync.RefRejectedError.
   158	//
   159	// When a single push rejects multiple refs, report-status surfaces the first
   160	// failing ref (go-git follows canonical git here), so Ref/Reason reflect that
   161	// first ref; any others resurface on the next attempt. The ErrTargetRefMoved
   162	// classification cannot be reproduced by external construction (the deciding
   163	// field is unexported by design) — to exercise errors.Is in a downstream test,
   164	// wrap the sentinel directly: fmt.Errorf("...: %w", gitsync.ErrTargetRefMoved).
   165	type RefRejectedError struct {
   166		Ref    string // the rejected ref, e.g. "refs/heads/main"
   167		Reason string // raw receive-pack ng reason, e.g. "remote ref has changed"
   168	
   169		moved bool  // git-sync's mode-independent judgment: an unambiguous concurrent move
   170		err   error // underlying error; preserves *packp.CommandStatusErr (+ any lease-hint annotation)
   171	}
   172	
   173	// Error is safe on a zero-value/externally-constructed RefRejectedError (one
   174	// with no wrapped err), so embedders can build &RefRejectedError{Ref, Reason}
   175	// in tests without a nil panic.
   176	func (e *RefRejectedError) Error() string {
   177		if e.err == nil {
   178			return fmt.Sprintf("ref %s rejected: %s", e.Ref, e.Reason)
   179		}
   180		return e.err.Error()
   181	}
   182	
   183	// Unwrap exposes the underlying receive-pack error so existing
   184	// errors.As(*packp.CommandStatusErr) checks — and substring inspection of the
   185	// message — keep working byte-for-byte for callers that have not migrated.
   186	func (e *RefRejectedError) Unwrap() error { return e.err }
   187	
   188	// Is matches ErrTargetRefMoved only when this rejection is a concurrent
   189	// target-ref move. Other rejections remain reachable via errors.As but are not
   190	// ErrTargetRefMoved.
   191	func (e *RefRejectedError) Is(target error) bool {
   192		return target == ErrTargetRefMoved && e.moved
   193	}
   194	
   195	// concurrentMoveMarkers are receive-pack ng reasons that UNAMBIGUOUSLY mean the
   196	// target ref changed under us between plan and push — a clean compare-and-swap /
   197	// lease miss that a plain retry resolves. Deliberately NARROWER than
   198	// leaseFailureMarkers: "non-fast-forward" / "fetch first" are excluded because an
   199	// update that is legitimately non-fast-forward and wasn't force-pushed looks
   200	// identical to a race, and treating it as a benign move would mask a real
   201	// "needs --force" failure.
   202	//
   203	// This set is server-specific by design. "remote ref has changed" is
   204	// entire-server's compare-and-swap rejection (storage.ErrReferenceHasChanged) —
   205	// the one git-sync's own targets emit, and the case that matters in practice.
   206	// "stale info" is git's force-with-lease lease-miss phrasing, kept for
   207	// consistency with leaseFailureMarkers and defence-in-depth; note it is
   208	// primarily a client-side status, so it may not arrive as a server ng reason on
   209	// every target. Stock git servers phrase a CAS miss differently again
   210	// ("failed to update ref" / "cannot lock ref ... but expected ..."), which this
   211	// set does NOT match — so against a non-entire target a genuine race may fall
   212	// through to a plain rejection. Extend this set as new server phrasings are
   213	// observed. Match is case-insensitive substring (Reason is free-form; see
   214	// RefRejectedError).
   215	var concurrentMoveMarkers = []string{
   216		"remote ref has changed",
   217		"stale info",
   218	}
   219	
   220	// isConcurrentMove reports whether a receive-pack ng reason is an unambiguous
   221	// concurrent target-ref move (see concurrentMoveMarkers).
   222	func isConcurrentMove(reason string) bool {
   223		lowered := strings.ToLower(reason)
   224		for _, marker := range concurrentMoveMarkers {
   225			if strings.Contains(lowered, marker) {
   226				return true
   227			}
   228		}
   229		return false
   230	}
   231	
   232	// asRefRejectedError wraps a target receive-pack report-status "ng" error in
   233	// a typed *RefRejectedError so callers can branch on errors.As /
   234	// errors.Is(err, ErrTargetRefMoved) instead of substring-matching the free-form
   235	// reason themselves. Inputs that are not a per-ref command status (e.g. an
   236	// unpack-status error) pass through unchanged. The input is preserved via Unwrap,
   237	// so the message and any errors.As(*packp.CommandStatusErr) check are unchanged.
   238	func asRefRejectedError(err error) error {
   239		var cs *packp.CommandStatusErr
   240		if !errors.As(err, &cs) {
   241			return err
   242		}
   243		return &RefRejectedError{
   244			Ref:    cs.ReferenceName.String(),
   245			Reason: cs.Status,
   246			moved:  isConcurrentMove(cs.Status),
   247			err:    err,
   248		}
   249	}
   250	
   251	// sendReceivePack encodes and POSTs a receive-pack request, then decodes the report.
   252	func sendReceivePack(
   253		ctx context.Context,
   254		conn Conn,
   255		req *packp.UpdateRequests,
   256		packData io.Reader,
   257		verbose bool,
   258		onRejection func(plumbing.ReferenceName, string),
   259	) error {
   260		var header bytes.Buffer
   261		if err := req.Encode(&header); err != nil {
   262			return fmt.Errorf("encode update-request: %w", err)
   263		}
   264		// The push body is io.MultiReader(header, packData); packData comes
   265		// from a live upload-pack pipe and isn't rewindable, so a mid-stream
   266		// 401 can't trigger PostRPCStreamBody's normal helper retry. Probe
   267		// for auth requirements with a same-shape POST first.
   268		if hc, ok := conn.(*HTTPConn); ok {
   269			hc.EnsureAuthForService(ctx, transport.ReceivePackService)
   270		}
   271		body := io.Reader(bytes.NewReader(header.Bytes()))
   272		if packData != nil {
   273			body = io.MultiReader(body, packData)
   274		}
   275		reader, err := PostRPCStreamBody(ctx, conn, transport.ReceivePackService, body, false, "receive-pack push")
   276		if err != nil {
   277			return fmt.Errorf("target receive-pack: %w", err)
   278		}
   279		defer reader.Close()
   280	
   281		// Unwrap sideband if negotiated; stream server-side progress to stderr
   282		// when verbose so long-running pushes show "Resolving deltas ..." etc.
   283		var respReader io.Reader = reader
   284		switch {
   285		case req.Capabilities.Supports(capability.Sideband64k):
   286			dem := sideband.NewDemuxer(sideband.Sideband64k, reader)
   287			dem.Progress = progressSink(verbose, "target: ", conn.ProgressWriter())
   288			respReader = dem
   289		case req.Capabilities.Supports(capability.Sideband):
   290			dem := sideband.NewDemuxer(sideband.Sideband, reader)
   291			dem.Progress = progressSink(verbose, "target: ", conn.ProgressWriter())
   292			respReader = dem
   293		}
   294	
   295		if req.Capabilities.Supports(capability.ReportStatus) {
   296			report := &packp.ReportStatus{}
   297			if err := report.Decode(respReader); err != nil {
   298				return fmt.Errorf("decode report-status: %w", err)
   299			}
   300			if onRejection == nil {
   301				if err := report.Error(); err != nil {
   302					return fmt.Errorf("report-status: %w", asRefRejectedError(annotateLeaseFailure(err)))
   303				}
   304				return nil
   305			}
   306			if report.UnpackStatus != "" && report.UnpackStatus != "ok" {
   307				return fmt.Errorf("report-status: unpack error: %s", report.UnpackStatus)
   308			}
   309			for _, cs := range report.CommandStatuses {
   310				if cs.Status == "" || cs.Status == "ok" {
   311					continue
   312				}
   313				onRejection(cs.ReferenceName, cs.Status)
   314			}
   315		}
   316		return nil
   317	}
   318	
   319	// PushObjects pushes locally-materialized objects to the target.
   320	//
   321	// Delta selection runs synchronously up front via
   322	// packfile.DeltaSelector. The selected objects are then handed back to
   323	// a packfile.Encoder behind a passthrough ObjectSelector, so the
   324	// encoder's write phase (Encode → encode(objects)) streams pack bytes
   325	// continuously into an io.Pipe to the HTTP request body. This avoids
   326	// the mid-stream stall that occurs when Encode runs selection itself —
   327	// CDN edges treat the resulting idle gap as a stalled upload and close
   328	// the connection. See go-git PR #2142 for the API hook.
   329	func PushObjects(
   330		ctx context.Context,
   331		conn Conn,
   332		adv *packp.AdvRefs,
   333		commands []PushCommand,
   334		store storer.Storer,
   335		hashes []plumbing.Hash,
   336		verbose bool,
   337		onRejection func(plumbing.ReferenceName, string),
   338	) error {
   339		req, _, hasUpdates, err := buildUpdateRequest(adv, commands, verbose)
   340		if err != nil {
   341			return err
   342		}
   343		if !hasUpdates {
   344			return sendReceivePack(ctx, conn, req, nil, verbose, onRejection)
   345		}
   346	
   347		progressDest := progressSink(verbose, "target: ", conn.ProgressWriter())
   348	
   349		stopSelect := startSelectionProgress(progressDest)
   350		objects, err := packfile.NewDeltaSelector(store).ObjectsToPack(hashes, 10)
   351		stopSelect(len(objects), err)
   352		if err != nil {
   353			return fmt.Errorf("select objects to pack: %w", err)
   354		}
   355	
   356		useRefDeltas := !adv.Capabilities.Supports(capability.OFSDelta)
   357		pr, pw := io.Pipe()
   358		done := make(chan error, 1)
   359		go func() {
   360			cw := &countingWriter{w: pw}
   361			stopWrite := startPackWriteProgress(cw, progressDest)
   362			defer stopWrite()
   363			enc := packfile.NewEncoder(cw, store, useRefDeltas,
   364				packfile.WithObjectSelector(precomputedSelector{objects: objects}))
   365			if _, err := enc.Encode(hashes, 10); err != nil {
   366				done <- pw.CloseWithError(fmt.Errorf("encode packfile: %w", err))
   367				return
   368			}
   369			done <- pw.Close()
   370		}()
   371	
   372		err = sendReceivePack(ctx, conn, req, pr, verbose, onRejection)
   373		_ = pr.Close()
   374		encodeErr := <-done
   375		if err != nil {
   376			return err
   377		}
   378		return encodeErr
   379	}
   380	
   381	// precomputedSelector is a packfile.ObjectSelector that returns a
   382	// fixed []*packfile.ObjectToPack, ignoring its arguments. It is the
   383	// passthrough used by PushObjects to feed pre-selected objects back
   384	// into packfile.Encoder via WithObjectSelector. Used exactly once per
   385	// PushObjects call and not exposed outside this package.
   386	type precomputedSelector struct {
   387		objects []*packfile.ObjectToPack
   388	}
   389	
   390	func (p precomputedSelector) ObjectsToPack(_ []plumbing.Hash, _ uint) ([]*packfile.ObjectToPack, error) {
   391		return p.objects, nil
   392	}
   393	
   394	// countingWriter wraps an io.Writer and tracks total bytes written.
   395	// The count is read by the progress ticker concurrently with the
   396	// encoder's writes, so the counter is atomic.
   397	type countingWriter struct {
   398		w io.Writer
   399		n atomic.Int64
   400	}
   401	
   402	func (cw *countingWriter) Write(p []byte) (int, error) {
   403		n, err := cw.w.Write(p)
   404		cw.n.Add(int64(n))
   405		if err != nil {
   406			return n, fmt.Errorf("counting writer: %w", err)
   407		}
   408		return n, nil
   409	}
   410	
   411	func (cw *countingWriter) Count() int64 { return cw.n.Load() }
   412	
   413	// startSelectionProgress emits in-place "selecting deltas, elapsed X"
   414	// updates every 500ms during the synchronous delta-selection phase of
   415	// PushObjects. The returned stop function takes the number of selected
   416	// objects and the selection error (nil on success); on success it
   417	// finalizes the line with a permanent "selected N objects in Y"
   418	// summary, on error it just stops the ticker without claiming success.
   419	// When dest is nil (non-verbose mode) returns a no-op stop, so
   420	// callers don't need to special-case verbosity.
   421	//
   422	// Selection has no observable byte progress — go-git's DeltaSelector
   423	// is opaque to the caller — so elapsed time is the only signal we can
   424	// surface to keep long selections from looking like a hang.
   425	func startSelectionProgress(dest io.Writer) func(objectCount int, err error) {
   426		if dest == nil {
   427			return func(int, error) {}
   428		}
   429		start := time.Now()
   430		ticker := time.NewTicker(500 * time.Millisecond)
   431		stop := make(chan struct{})
   432		done := make(chan struct{})
   433		go func() {
   434			defer close(done)
   435			for {
   436				select {
   437				case <-stop:
   438					return
   439				case <-ticker.C:
   440					fmt.Fprintf(dest, "selecting deltas, elapsed %s\r",
   441						time.Since(start).Round(time.Second))
   442				}
   443			}
   444		}()
   445		return func(objectCount int, err error) {
   446			ticker.Stop()
   447			close(stop)
   448			<-done
   449			if err != nil {
   450				return
   451			}
   452			fmt.Fprintf(dest, "selected %d objects in %s\n",
   453				objectCount, time.Since(start).Round(time.Second))
   454		}
   455	}
   456	
   457	// startPackWriteProgress emits in-place "encoding pack: N MB, elapsed
   458	// X" updates every 500ms while the encoder writes pack bytes through
   459	// cw. The returned stop function finalizes the line with a permanent
   460	// "encoded pack" summary. Single-use, typically via defer. When dest
   461	// is nil returns a no-op stop.
   462	//
   463	// This is the second of two phases visible to a materialized push:
   464	// startSelectionProgress runs synchronously first, then
   465	// startPackWriteProgress takes over once selection has completed and
   466	// the encoder begins streaming bytes to the request body.
   467	func startPackWriteProgress(cw *countingWriter, dest io.Writer) func() {
   468		if dest == nil {
   469			return func() {}
   470		}
   471		start := time.Now()
   472		ticker := time.NewTicker(500 * time.Millisecond)
   473		stop := make(chan struct{})
   474		done := make(chan struct{})
   475		go func() {
   476			defer close(done)
   477			for {
   478				select {
   479				case <-stop:
   480					return
   481				case <-ticker.C:
   482					fmt.Fprintf(dest, "encoding pack: %s, elapsed %s\r",
   483						humanizeBytes(cw.Count()), time.Since(start).Round(time.Second))
   484				}
   485			}
   486		}()
   487		return func() {
   488			ticker.Stop()
   489			close(stop)
   490			<-done
   491			fmt.Fprintf(dest, "encoded pack: %s in %s\n",
   492				humanizeBytes(cw.Count()), time.Since(start).Round(time.Second))
   493		}
   494	}
   495	
   496	// humanizeBytes renders n in IEC units with one decimal place for KB+
   497	// (e.g. "47.3 MB"). Anything below 1 KB is shown as raw bytes.
   498	func humanizeBytes(n int64) string {
   499		const (
   500			kb = 1024
   501			mb = kb * 1024
   502			gb = mb * 1024
   503		)
   504		switch {
   505		case n < kb:
   506			return fmt.Sprintf("%d B", n)
   507		case n < mb:
   508			return fmt.Sprintf("%.1f KB", float64(n)/float64(kb))
   509		case n < gb:
   510			return fmt.Sprintf("%.1f MB", float64(n)/float64(mb))
   511		default:
   512			return fmt.Sprintf("%.1f GB", float64(n)/float64(gb))
   513		}
   514	}
   515	
   516	// PushPack pushes a pack stream (relay) to the target.
   517	func PushPack(
   518		ctx context.Context,
   519		conn Conn,
   520		adv *packp.AdvRefs,
   521		commands []PushCommand,
   522		pack io.ReadCloser,
   523		verbose bool,
   524		onRejection func(plumbing.ReferenceName, string),
   525	) error {
   526		for _, cmd := range commands {
   527			if cmd.Delete {
   528				_ = pack.Close()
   529				return errors.New("pack push only supports create and update actions")
   530			}
   531		}
   532	
   533		req, _, _, err := buildUpdateRequest(adv, commands, verbose)
   534		if err != nil {
   535			_ = pack.Close()
   536			return err
   537		}
   538	
   539		err = sendReceivePack(ctx, conn, req, pack, verbose, onRejection)
   540		closeErr := pack.Close()
   541		if err != nil {
   542			return err
   543		}
   544		if closeErr != nil {
   545			return fmt.Errorf("close pack: %w", closeErr)
   546		}
   547		return nil
   548	}
   549	
   550	// PushCommands sends ref update commands without a pack (for ref-only changes).
   551	func PushCommands(
   552		ctx context.Context,
   553		conn Conn,
   554		adv *packp.AdvRefs,
   555		commands []PushCommand,
   556		verbose bool,
   557		onRejection func(plumbing.ReferenceName, string),
   558	) error {
   559		req, _, _, err := buildUpdateRequest(adv, commands, verbose)
   560		if err != nil {
   561			return err
   562		}
   563		return sendReceivePack(ctx, conn, req, nil, verbose, onRejection)
   564	}
   565	
   566	func progressWriter(verbose bool, dest io.Writer) io.Writer {
   567		if !verbose {
   568			return nil
   569		}
   570		if dest == nil {
   571			dest = os.Stderr
   572		}
   573		return dest
   574	}
   575	
   576	// progressSink returns a line-prefixing io.Writer suitable for
   577	// sideband.Demuxer.Progress. When verbose is false it returns nil so the
   578	// demuxer discards progress frames without allocating. Passing a non-nil
   579	// dest routes the prefixed lines through that writer instead of os.Stderr,
   580	// which lets a live progress reporter coordinate output.
   581	func progressSink(verbose bool, prefix string, dest io.Writer) io.Writer {
   582		if !verbose {
   583			return nil
   584		}
   585		if dest == nil {
   586			dest = os.Stderr
   587		}
   588		return &prefixedLineWriter{w: dest, prefix: prefix, atLineStart: true}
   589	}
   590	
   591	// prefixedLineWriter prepends a fixed prefix to each line of input written
   592	// to the wrapped writer. Git sideband progress arrives as chunks that may
   593	// contain '\n' between full lines or '\r' for in-place updates ("Resolving
   594	// deltas:  12%\r"); both are treated as line terminators so the next chunk
   595	// gets a fresh prefix.
   596	type prefixedLineWriter struct {
   597		w           io.Writer
   598		prefix      string
   599		atLineStart bool
   600	}
   601	
   602	func (p *prefixedLineWriter) Write(b []byte) (int, error) {
   603		consumed := 0
   604		for len(b) > 0 {
   605			if p.atLineStart {
   606				if _, err := io.WriteString(p.w, p.prefix); err != nil {
   607					return consumed, fmt.Errorf("write prefix: %w", err)
   608				}
   609				p.atLineStart = false
   610			}
   611			i := bytes.IndexAny(b, "\r\n")
   612			var chunk []byte
   613			if i < 0 {
   614				chunk = b
   615			} else {
   616				chunk = b[:i+1]
   617				p.atLineStart = true
   618			}
   619			n, err := p.w.Write(chunk)
   620			consumed += n
   621			if err != nil {
   622				return consumed, fmt.Errorf("write chunk: %w", err)
   623			}
   624			b = b[len(chunk):]
   625		}
   626		return consumed, nil
   627	}

   104	// leaseFailureMarkers are receive-pack ng reason substrings that indicate the
   105	// captured target tip didn't match what was on the server at push time. Match
   106	// is case-insensitive. CommandStatusErr.Status is a free-form string in go-git,
   107	// so substring matching is the only option absent upstream sentinels.
   108	var leaseFailureMarkers = []string{
   109		"stale info",
   110		"fetch first",
   111		"non-fast-forward",
   112		"does not match",
   113	}
   114	
   115	// IsLeaseFailure reports whether a receive-pack ng reason indicates the
   116	// captured target tip no longer matched at push time. Callers that downgrade
   117	// per-ref rejections to warnings (BestEffort) must still treat these as fatal
   118	// to preserve --force-with-lease semantics.
   119	func IsLeaseFailure(status string) bool {
   120		lowered := strings.ToLower(status)
   121		for _, marker := range leaseFailureMarkers {
   122			if strings.Contains(lowered, marker) {
   123				return true
   124			}
   125		}
   126		return false
   127	}

   129	// annotateLeaseFailure wraps a lease-failure CommandStatusErr with a retry/
   130	// override hint. Other receive-pack errors pass through unchanged.
   131	func annotateLeaseFailure(err error) error {

   134			return err
   135		}

   137			return err
   138		}
   139		return fmt.Errorf("%w (target ref %s moved or differs from session start; rerun, or use --force-blind to overwrite)", err, cs.ReferenceName)
   140	}

   146	// error in the chain is a *RefRejectedError. Re-exported publicly as
   147	// gitsync.ErrTargetRefMoved.

   150	// RefRejectedError is a single per-ref "ng" status returned by the target's
   151	// receive-pack report-status. Ref is the rejected ref; Reason is the raw,
   152	// server-defined reason text — the git wire protocol carries no structured error
   153	// code, so Reason is free-form and server-specific. Reach it with errors.As.

   195	// concurrentMoveMarkers are receive-pack ng reasons that UNAMBIGUOUSLY mean the
   196	// target ref changed under us between plan and push — a clean compare-and-swap /

   213	// observed. Match is case-insensitive substring (Reason is free-form; see
   214	// RefRejectedError).
   215	var concurrentMoveMarkers = []string{
   216		"remote ref has changed",
   217		"stale info",
   218	}

   220	// isConcurrentMove reports whether a receive-pack ng reason is an unambiguous
   221	// concurrent target-ref move (see concurrentMoveMarkers).
   222	func isConcurrentMove(reason string) bool {
   223		lowered := strings.ToLower(reason)

   230	}
   231	
   232	// asRefRejectedError wraps a target receive-pack report-status "ng" error in
   233	// a typed *RefRejectedError so callers can branch on errors.As /
   234	// errors.Is(err, ErrTargetRefMoved) instead of substring-matching the free-form
   235	// reason themselves. Inputs that are not a per-ref command status (e.g. an
   236	// unpack-status error) pass through unchanged. The input is preserved via Unwrap,
   237	// so the message and any errors.As(*packp.CommandStatusErr) check are unchanged.
   238	func asRefRejectedError(err error) error {
   239		var cs *packp.CommandStatusErr
   240		if !errors.As(err, &cs) {
   241			return err
   242		}

   244			Ref:    cs.ReferenceName.String(),
   245			Reason: cs.Status,
   246			moved:  isConcurrentMove(cs.Status),

   248		}
   249	}
