   251	// sendReceivePack encodes and POSTs a receive-pack request, then decodes the report.
   252	func sendReceivePack(
   253		ctx context.Context,
   254		conn Conn,
   255		req *packp.UpdateRequests,
   256		packData io.Reader,
   257		verbose bool,
   258		onRejection func(plumbing.ReferenceName, string),
   259	) error {
   260		var header bytes.Buffer
   261		if err := req.Encode(&header); err != nil {
   262			return fmt.Errorf("encode update-request: %w", err)
   263		}
   264		// The push body is io.MultiReader(header, packData); packData comes
   265		// from a live upload-pack pipe and isn't rewindable, so a mid-stream
   266		// 401 can't trigger PostRPCStreamBody's normal helper retry. Probe
   267		// for auth requirements with a same-shape POST first.
   268		if hc, ok := conn.(*HTTPConn); ok {
   269			hc.EnsureAuthForService(ctx, transport.ReceivePackService)
   270		}
   271		body := io.Reader(bytes.NewReader(header.Bytes()))
   272		if packData != nil {
   273			body = io.MultiReader(body, packData)
   274		}
   275		reader, err := PostRPCStreamBody(ctx, conn, transport.ReceivePackService, body, false, "receive-pack push")
   276		if err != nil {
   277			return fmt.Errorf("target receive-pack: %w", err)
   278		}
   279		defer reader.Close()
   280	
   281		// Unwrap sideband if negotiated; stream server-side progress to stderr
   282		// when verbose so long-running pushes show "Resolving deltas ..." etc.
   283		var respReader io.Reader = reader
   284		switch {
   285		case req.Capabilities.Supports(capability.Sideband64k):
   286			dem := sideband.NewDemuxer(sideband.Sideband64k, reader)
   287			dem.Progress = progressSink(verbose, "target: ", conn.ProgressWriter())
   288			respReader = dem
   289		case req.Capabilities.Supports(capability.Sideband):
   290			dem := sideband.NewDemuxer(sideband.Sideband, reader)
   291			dem.Progress = progressSink(verbose, "target: ", conn.ProgressWriter())
   292			respReader = dem
   293		}
   294	
   295		if req.Capabilities.Supports(capability.ReportStatus) {
   296			report := &packp.ReportStatus{}
   297			if err := report.Decode(respReader); err != nil {
   298				return fmt.Errorf("decode report-status: %w", err)
   299			}
   300			if onRejection == nil {
   301				if err := report.Error(); err != nil {
   302					return fmt.Errorf("report-status: %w", asRefRejectedError(annotateLeaseFailure(err)))
   303				}
   304				return nil
   305			}
   306			if report.UnpackStatus != "" && report.UnpackStatus != "ok" {
   307				return fmt.Errorf("report-status: unpack error: %s", report.UnpackStatus)
   308			}
   309			for _, cs := range report.CommandStatuses {
   310				if cs.Status == "" || cs.Status == "ok" {
   311					continue
   312				}
   313				onRejection(cs.ReferenceName, cs.Status)
   314			}
   315		}
   316		return nil
   317	}
   318	
   319	// PushObjects pushes locally-materialized objects to the target.
   320	//
   321	// Delta selection runs synchronously up front via
   322	// packfile.DeltaSelector. The selected objects are then handed back to
   323	// a packfile.Encoder behind a passthrough ObjectSelector, so the
   324	// encoder's write phase (Encode → encode(objects)) streams pack bytes
   325	// continuously into an io.Pipe to the HTTP request body. This avoids
   326	// the mid-stream stall that occurs when Encode runs selection itself —
   327	// CDN edges treat the resulting idle gap as a stalled upload and close
   328	// the connection. See go-git PR #2142 for the API hook.
   329	func PushObjects(
   330		ctx context.Context,
   331		conn Conn,
   332		adv *packp.AdvRefs,
   333		commands []PushCommand,
   334		store storer.Storer,
   335		hashes []plumbing.Hash,
   336		verbose bool,
   337		onRejection func(plumbing.ReferenceName, string),
   338	) error {
   339		req, _, hasUpdates, err := buildUpdateRequest(adv, commands, verbose)
   340		if err != nil {
   341			return err
   342		}
   343		if !hasUpdates {
   344			return sendReceivePack(ctx, conn, req, nil, verbose, onRejection)
   345		}
   346	
   347		progressDest := progressSink(verbose, "target: ", conn.ProgressWriter())
   348	
   349		stopSelect := startSelectionProgress(progressDest)
   350		objects, err := packfile.NewDeltaSelector(store).ObjectsToPack(hashes, 10)
   351		stopSelect(len(objects), err)
   352		if err != nil {
   353			return fmt.Errorf("select objects to pack: %w", err)
   354		}
   355	
   356		useRefDeltas := !adv.Capabilities.Supports(capability.OFSDelta)
   357		pr, pw := io.Pipe()
   358		done := make(chan error, 1)
   359		go func() {
   360			cw := &countingWriter{w: pw}
   361			stopWrite := startPackWriteProgress(cw, progressDest)
   362			defer stopWrite()
   363			enc := packfile.NewEncoder(cw, store, useRefDeltas,
   364				packfile.WithObjectSelector(precomputedSelector{objects: objects}))
   365			if _, err := enc.Encode(hashes, 10); err != nil {
   366				done <- pw.CloseWithError(fmt.Errorf("encode packfile: %w", err))
   367				return
   368			}
   369			done <- pw.Close()
   370		}()
   371	
   372		err = sendReceivePack(ctx, conn, req, pr, verbose, onRejection)
   373		_ = pr.Close()

   375		if err != nil {
   376			return err
   377		}
   378		return encodeErr
   379	}
   380	
   381	// precomputedSelector is a packfile.ObjectSelector that returns a
   382	// fixed []*packfile.ObjectToPack, ignoring its arguments. It is the
   383	// passthrough used by PushObjects to feed pre-selected objects back
   384	// into packfile.Encoder via WithObjectSelector. Used exactly once per
   385	// PushObjects call and not exposed outside this package.
   386	type precomputedSelector struct {
   387		objects []*packfile.ObjectToPack
   388	}
   389	
   390	func (p precomputedSelector) ObjectsToPack(_ []plumbing.Hash, _ uint) ([]*packfile.ObjectToPack, error) {
   391		return p.objects, nil
   392	}
   393	
   394	// countingWriter wraps an io.Writer and tracks total bytes written.
   395	// The count is read by the progress ticker concurrently with the
   396	// encoder's writes, so the counter is atomic.
   397	type countingWriter struct {
   398		w io.Writer
   399		n atomic.Int64
   400	}
   401	
   402	func (cw *countingWriter) Write(p []byte) (int, error) {
   403		n, err := cw.w.Write(p)
   404		cw.n.Add(int64(n))
   405		if err != nil {
   406			return n, fmt.Errorf("counting writer: %w", err)
   407		}
   408		return n, nil
   409	}
   410	
   411	func (cw *countingWriter) Count() int64 { return cw.n.Load() }
   412	
   413	// startSelectionProgress emits in-place "selecting deltas, elapsed X"
   414	// updates every 500ms during the synchronous delta-selection phase of
   415	// PushObjects. The returned stop function takes the number of selected
   416	// objects and the selection error (nil on success); on success it
   417	// finalizes the line with a permanent "selected N objects in Y"
   418	// summary, on error it just stops the ticker without claiming success.
   419	// When dest is nil (non-verbose mode) returns a no-op stop, so
   420	// callers don't need to special-case verbosity.
   421	//
   422	// Selection has no observable byte progress — go-git's DeltaSelector
   423	// is opaque to the caller — so elapsed time is the only signal we can
   424	// surface to keep long selections from looking like a hang.
   425	func startSelectionProgress(dest io.Writer) func(objectCount int, err error) {
   426		if dest == nil {
   427			return func(int, error) {}
   428		}
   429		start := time.Now()
   430		ticker := time.NewTicker(500 * time.Millisecond)
   431		stop := make(chan struct{})
   432		done := make(chan struct{})
   433		go func() {
   434			defer close(done)
   435			for {
   436				select {
   437				case <-stop:
   438					return
   439				case <-ticker.C:
   440					fmt.Fprintf(dest, "selecting deltas, elapsed %s\r",
   441						time.Since(start).Round(time.Second))
   442				}
   443			}
   444		}()
   445		return func(objectCount int, err error) {
   446			ticker.Stop()
   447			close(stop)
   448			<-done
   449			if err != nil {
   450				return
   451			}
   452			fmt.Fprintf(dest, "selected %d objects in %s\n",
   453				objectCount, time.Since(start).Round(time.Second))
   454		}
   455	}
   456	
   457	// startPackWriteProgress emits in-place "encoding pack: N MB, elapsed
   458	// X" updates every 500ms while the encoder writes pack bytes through
   459	// cw. The returned stop function finalizes the line with a permanent
   460	// "encoded pack" summary. Single-use, typically via defer. When dest
   461	// is nil returns a no-op stop.
   462	//
   463	// This is the second of two phases visible to a materialized push:
   464	// startSelectionProgress runs synchronously first, then
   465	// startPackWriteProgress takes over once selection has completed and
   466	// the encoder begins streaming bytes to the request body.
   467	func startPackWriteProgress(cw *countingWriter, dest io.Writer) func() {
   468		if dest == nil {
   469			return func() {}
   470		}
   471		start := time.Now()
   472		ticker := time.NewTicker(500 * time.Millisecond)
   473		stop := make(chan struct{})
   474		done := make(chan struct{})
   475		go func() {
   476			defer close(done)
   477			for {
   478				select {
   479				case <-stop:
   480					return
   481				case <-ticker.C:
   482					fmt.Fprintf(dest, "encoding pack: %s, elapsed %s\r",
   483						humanizeBytes(cw.Count()), time.Since(start).Round(time.Second))
   484				}
   485			}
   486		}()
   487		return func() {
   488			ticker.Stop()
   489			close(stop)
   490			<-done
   491			fmt.Fprintf(dest, "encoded pack: %s in %s\n",
   492				humanizeBytes(cw.Count()), time.Since(start).Round(time.Second))
   493		}
   494	}
   495	
   496	// humanizeBytes renders n in IEC units with one decimal place for KB+
   497	// (e.g. "47.3 MB"). Anything below 1 KB is shown as raw bytes.
   498	func humanizeBytes(n int64) string {
   499		const (
   500			kb = 1024
   501			mb = kb * 1024
   502			gb = mb * 1024
   503		)
   504		switch {
   505		case n < kb:
   506			return fmt.Sprintf("%d B", n)
   507		case n < mb:
   508			return fmt.Sprintf("%.1f KB", float64(n)/float64(kb))
   509		case n < gb:
   510			return fmt.Sprintf("%.1f MB", float64(n)/float64(mb))
   511		default:
   512			return fmt.Sprintf("%.1f GB", float64(n)/float64(gb))
   513		}
   514	}
   515	
   516	// PushPack pushes a pack stream (relay) to the target.
   517	func PushPack(
   518		ctx context.Context,
   519		conn Conn,
   520		adv *packp.AdvRefs,
   521		commands []PushCommand,
   522		pack io.ReadCloser,
   523		verbose bool,
   524		onRejection func(plumbing.ReferenceName, string),
   525	) error {
   526		for _, cmd := range commands {
   527			if cmd.Delete {
   528				_ = pack.Close()
   529				return errors.New("pack push only supports create and update actions")
   530			}
   531		}
   532	
   533		req, _, _, err := buildUpdateRequest(adv, commands, verbose)
   534		if err != nil {
   535			_ = pack.Close()
   536			return err
   537		}
   538	
   539		err = sendReceivePack(ctx, conn, req, pack, verbose, onRejection)
   540		closeErr := pack.Close()
   541		if err != nil {
   542			return err
   543		}
   544		if closeErr != nil {
   545			return fmt.Errorf("close pack: %w", closeErr)
   546		}
   547		return nil
   548	}
   549	
   550	// PushCommands sends ref update commands without a pack (for ref-only changes).
   551	func PushCommands(
   552		ctx context.Context,
   553		conn Conn,
   554		adv *packp.AdvRefs,
   555		commands []PushCommand,
   556		verbose bool,
   557		onRejection func(plumbing.ReferenceName, string),
   558	) error {
   559		req, _, _, err := buildUpdateRequest(adv, commands, verbose)
   560		if err != nil {
   561			return err
   562		}
   563		return sendReceivePack(ctx, conn, req, nil, verbose, onRejection)
   564	}
   565	
   566	func progressWriter(verbose bool, dest io.Writer) io.Writer {
   567		if !verbose {
   568			return nil
   569		}
   570		if dest == nil {
   571			dest = os.Stderr
   572		}
   573		return dest
   574	}
   575	
   576	// progressSink returns a line-prefixing io.Writer suitable for
   577	// sideband.Demuxer.Progress. When verbose is false it returns nil so the
   578	// demuxer discards progress frames without allocating. Passing a non-nil
   579	// dest routes the prefixed lines through that writer instead of os.Stderr,
   580	// which lets a live progress reporter coordinate output.
   581	func progressSink(verbose bool, prefix string, dest io.Writer) io.Writer {
   582		if !verbose {
   583			return nil
   584		}
   585		if dest == nil {
   586			dest = os.Stderr
   587		}
   588		return &prefixedLineWriter{w: dest, prefix: prefix, atLineStart: true}
   589	}
   590	
   591	// prefixedLineWriter prepends a fixed prefix to each line of input written
   592	// to the wrapped writer. Git sideband progress arrives as chunks that may
   593	// contain '\n' between full lines or '\r' for in-place updates ("Resolving
   594	// deltas:  12%\r"); both are treated as line terminators so the next chunk
   595	// gets a fresh prefix.
   596	type prefixedLineWriter struct {
   597		w           io.Writer
   598		prefix      string
   599		atLineStart bool
   600	}
   601	
   602	func (p *prefixedLineWriter) Write(b []byte) (int, error) {
   603		consumed := 0
   604		for len(b) > 0 {
   605			if p.atLineStart {
   606				if _, err := io.WriteString(p.w, p.prefix); err != nil {
   607					return consumed, fmt.Errorf("write prefix: %w", err)
   608				}
   609				p.atLineStart = false
   610			}
   611			i := bytes.IndexAny(b, "\r\n")
   612			var chunk []byte
   613			if i < 0 {
   614				chunk = b
   615			} else {
   616				chunk = b[:i+1]
   617				p.atLineStart = true
   618			}
   619			n, err := p.w.Write(chunk)
   620			consumed += n
   621			if err != nil {
   622				return consumed, fmt.Errorf("write chunk: %w", err)
   623			}
   624			b = b[len(chunk):]
   625		}
   626		return consumed, nil
   627	}

     1	package main
     2	
     3	import (
     4		"errors"
     5		"regexp"
     6		"strings"
     7	
     8		gitsync "entire.io/entire/git-sync"
     9	)
    10	
    11	type syncError struct {
    12		err       error
    13		permanent bool
    14	}
    15	
    16	func (e *syncError) Error() string { return e.err.Error() }
    17	func (e *syncError) Unwrap() error { return e.err }
    18	
    19	func permanentErr(err error) error { return &syncError{err: err, permanent: true} }
    20	func transientErr(err error) error { return &syncError{err: err, permanent: false} }
    21	
    22	// errSourceNoRefs signals that a sync did nothing because the source has no
    23	// refs to mirror — an empty repository. It is benign, not a failure: the
    24	// worker acks the message (so JetStream doesn't redeliver a job that can only
    25	// ever no-op against an unchanged empty source) but records it as outcomeNoop
    26	// so a persistently-empty source stays visible rather than masquerading as a
    27	// healthy ref-applying sync. See disposeResult / classifyDisposition.
    28	var errSourceNoRefs = errors.New("source has no refs to mirror")
    29	
    30	// isNoSourceRefsError reports whether err is git-sync's "no source refs
    31	// matched" outcome. git-sync raises this when the desired ref set is empty;
    32	// it collapses "empty source" and "selection matched nothing" into the same
    33	// unexported error string. The worker syncs with AllRefs and no branch
    34	// selector or ref mappings (see syncer.go), so the only way the desired set
    35	// comes back empty is a genuinely empty source — which is a clean no-op, not a
    36	// failure. There is no exported sentinel to errors.Is against, so we match the
    37	// stable message substring (consistent with permanentStatusHints below).
    38	func isNoSourceRefsError(err error) bool {
    39		return err != nil && strings.Contains(err.Error(), "no source refs matched")
    40	}
    41	
    42	// isTransientGitFetchError reports whether err matches the intermittent
    43	// "http 404" pattern emitted by git-sync's transport on GitHub ref-discovery
    44	// failures. These have been observed (commit 9751992 investigation) to
    45	// succeed on retry while the same repo and installation remain healthy.
    46	//
    47	// Mint-time 404s ("GitHub API returned 404: ...") are NOT classified as
    48	// transient here — those represent a genuine installation/scope failure and
    49	// should surface as permanent errors after the first attempt.
    50	func isTransientGitFetchError(err error) bool {
    51		if err == nil {
    52			return false
    53		}
    54		msg := err.Error()
    55		if !strings.Contains(msg, "http 404") {
    56			return false
    57		}
    58		if strings.Contains(msg, "GitHub API returned") {
    59			return false
    60		}
    61		return true
    62	}
    63	
    64	// isConcurrentRefError reports whether err is a benign "target ref moved
    65	// concurrently" rejection — almost always a second sync of the same repo landing
    66	// first (rapid back-to-back webhooks, or a webhook overlapping the 15-min
    67	// rekicker). It is a clean compare-and-swap miss, not a real failure: the next
    68	// delivery re-plans against the new tip and applies (it usually no-ops, because
    69	// the job that won the race already mirrored the same source state). The
    70	// disposition layer (classifyDisposition) routes these to dispositionRaced so
    71	// they redeliver like a nak but stay out of the sync-failure alert's nak+term
    72	// numerator.
    73	//
    74	// We rely entirely on the typed gitsync.ErrTargetRefMoved sentinel; git-sync
    75	// owns the classification. Against entire-server targets a race always surfaces
    76	// as its compare-and-swap reason ("remote ref has changed"), which git-sync
    77	// wraps as this sentinel — so no worker-side substring matching is needed, and
    78	// none is done (a "non-fast-forward" rejection here would mean the source
    79	// rewound and needs a force the worker doesn't do, NOT a race). If a non-entire
    80	// target is ever introduced whose receive-pack phrases CAS misses differently,
    81	// broaden git-sync's marker set rather than re-adding matching here.
    82	//
    83	// These are a strict subset of the transient class (classifySyncError never
    84	// marks them permanent), so isPermanentError takes precedence in
    85	// classifyDisposition.
    86	func isConcurrentRefError(err error) bool {
    87		return errors.Is(err, gitsync.ErrTargetRefMoved)
    88	}
    89	
    90	// shouldMarkSpanError reports whether a sync error should surface as an error on
    91	// the mirror.consume APM span. It mirrors the disposition layer's notion of
    92	// "failure" so the trace error rate doesn't disagree with the outcome metric.
    93	// Two benign cases are excluded: an empty source (errSourceNoRefs, acked as a
    94	// no-op) and a concurrent-ref race that will redeliver and self-heal
    95	// (dispositionRaced). A race that has EXHAUSTED redelivery (lastDelivery) is no
    96	// longer benign — disposeResult escalates it to term — so it stays marked.
    97	// Pure (no span/IO) so the decision is unit-testable; the handler owns the
    98	// span side effects.
    99	func shouldMarkSpanError(err error, lastDelivery bool) bool {
   100		if err == nil {
   101			return false
   102		}
   103		if errors.Is(err, errSourceNoRefs) {
   104			return false
   105		}
   106		if isConcurrentRefError(err) && !lastDelivery {
   107			return false
   108		}
   109		return true
   110	}
   111	
   112	// authHeaderPattern matches Basic/Bearer Authorization header values that can
   113	// end up echoed in error response bodies. git-sync's transport error
   114	// formatter (vendor .../gitproto/smarthttp.go) includes up to 64KB of the
   115	// response body in its error string, so a malicious or buggy upstream that
   116	// reflects the request Authorization header would leak the installation
   117	// token (base64-encoded for Basic). github.com itself doesn't do this, but
   118	// the cost of defence-in-depth here is tiny.
   119	var authHeaderPattern = regexp.MustCompile(`(?i)(Basic|Bearer)\s+[A-Za-z0-9+/=_\-\.]+`)
   120	
   121	// redactAuth replaces Authorization header values found in an error message
   122	// with a fixed placeholder, dropping any wrap chain so the sensitive original
   123	// cannot be recovered through errors.Unwrap.
   124	func redactAuth(err error) error {
   125		if err == nil {
   126			return nil
   127		}
   128		redacted := authHeaderPattern.ReplaceAllString(err.Error(), "$1 <redacted>")
   129		if redacted == err.Error() {
   130			return err
   131		}
   132		return errors.New(redacted)
   133	}
   134	
   135	// permanentStatusHints match any response body that reveals a 4xx from either
   136	// git-sync's transport error formatter ("http 401: ..."), the old direct-mint
   137	// path's GitHub error formatter ("GitHub API returned 422: ..."), or
   138	// coreapp.Minter's STS error formatter ("sts/token returned 403"). All of
   139	// these map to "retrying will not help" — revoked install, private-repo
   140	// without auth, repo not on the installation, wrong name, or entire-core
   141	// refusing the mint because the mirror metadata doesn't match the request.
   142	//
   143	// "http 404" is permanent at this layer because the intermittent GitHub-side
   144	// 404s are already retried in-process by sync() (see maxSyncAttempts). Any
   145	// 404 that reaches classification has already survived those retries, so
   146	// NATS redelivery would just repeat work.
   147	var permanentStatusHints = []string{
   148		"http 401", "http 403", "http 404",
   149		"GitHub API returned 401",
   150		"GitHub API returned 403",
   151		"GitHub API returned 404",
   152		"GitHub API returned 422",
   153		"sts/token returned 401",
   154		"sts/token returned 403",
   155		"sts/token returned 404",
   156	}
   157	
   158	// classifySyncError maps an error to a permanent or transient syncError based
   159	// on whether the underlying HTTP status suggests the work can ever succeed.
   160	func classifySyncError(err error) error {
   161		msg := err.Error()
   162		for _, hint := range permanentStatusHints {
   163			if strings.Contains(msg, hint) {
   164				return permanentErr(err)
   165			}
   166		}
   167		return transientErr(err)
   168	}

content.text: The `redactAuth` function is a critical detail: it flattens the error to `errors.New(redacted)` (dropping the wrap chain) **only when the auth pattern matches**, otherwise returns the original error untouched. Let me trace the full consumer flow — how `err` flows from `sync()` through `redactAuth`/`classifySyncError` into both the span-marking and `disposeResult`, and confirm `isLastDelivery` is consistent across the two call sites.

1		if parsed.SourceKind != gitjobs.SourceKindGithub {
       2			err := permanentErr(fmt.Errorf("unsupported git source kind %q", parsed.SourceKind))
       3			span.RecordError(err)
       4			span.SetStatus(otelcodes.Error, "unsupported git source")
       5			span.SetAttributes(
       6				attribute.Bool("entire.error.permanent", true),
       7				attribute.Bool("entire.message.terminated", true),
       8			)
       9			slog.ErrorContext(ctx, "Unsupported git source, terminating", slog.String("source_kind", string(parsed.SourceKind)))
      10			c.terminate(ctx, msg, j.Target.ULID, msg.Subject, msg.Data, failindex.ReasonValidation, failindex.SourceWorkerValidation, err)
      11			return
      12		}
      13	
      14		installationID, err := gitjobs.ParseGithubInstallationAuthRef(j.Source.AuthRef)
      15		if err != nil {
      16			err := permanentErr(err)
      17			span.RecordError(err)
      18			span.SetStatus(otelcodes.Error, "invalid git source auth ref")
      19			span.SetAttributes(
      20				attribute.Bool("entire.error.permanent", true),
      21				attribute.Bool("entire.message.terminated", true),
      22			)
      23			slog.ErrorContext(ctx, "Invalid git source auth ref, terminating", slog.Any("error", err))
      24			c.terminate(ctx, msg, j.Target.ULID, msg.Subject, msg.Data, failindex.ReasonValidation, failindex.SourceWorkerValidation, err)
      25			return
      26		}
      27		span.SetAttributes(attribute.Int64("entire.installation_id", installationID))
      28	
      29		// A single sync on a multi-GB repo regularly exceeds consumerAckWait, so
      30		// extend the ack deadline on a sub-AckWait cadence while sync runs. The
      31		// extender's lifetime is exactly the sync call: explicit stop right after
      32		// sync returns keeps it from overlapping with Ack/Term/Nak in
      33		// disposeResult (an InProgress racing an Ack would log a spurious warn),
      34		// and a deferred stop covers the panic path. stopExtender is idempotent,
      35		// so the two paths don't fight. We deliberately don't raise AckWait: if a
      36		// pod dies mid-job we still want redelivery within the configured window.
      37		stopExtender := startInProgressExtender(ctx, msg, consumerInProgressInterval)
      38		defer stopExtender()
      39	
      40		// Bound the attempt. git-sync is context-aware end to end (every HTTP
      41		// request is built with NewRequestWithContext), so the deadline aborts
      42		// in-flight transfers — which also stops the extender above, restoring
      43		// AckWait expiry as the backstop. A timeout classifies as transient →
      44		// Nak; a repo that exceeds the budget on every attempt exhausts
      45		// MaxDeliver and lands in the failure index (reason=max_deliveries).
      46		syncCtx := ctx

      91	func (c *consumer) disposeResult(ctx context.Context, m ackableMsg, err error, provider, ulid, subject string, payload []byte, viaRekick bool) {

      93		switch classifyDisposition(err) {

     101			// nothing (it is deliberately not a ref-applying success — see
     102			// last_success_unix), so it must not prune a recorded terminal failure
     103			// that may have left the mirror inconsistent. Only a real sync (Ack) or a
     104			// re-kick that ends in one clears. A genuinely-emptied source self-heals
     105			// on its next real sync, or the entry ages out via the bucket ttl.
     106			c.metrics.recordSyncOutcome(ctx, provider, c.jurisdiction, outcomeNoop)
     107			_ = m.Ack()
     108		case dispositionTerm:
     109			// Best-effort record, then Term regardless — fail open. recordTerminal
     110			// logs + counts record_failed{cause} on a KV write failure; we Term
     111			// either way rather than Nak, because the failure is permanent (re-running
     112			// the sync would be wasted work) and a KV outage must not wedge the
     113			// consumer. Recovery of a dropped record is owned by the record_failed
     114			// alert + L2 reconcile, not by holding the message.
     115			c.recordTerminal(ctx, ulid, subject, payload, seq, failindex.ReasonPermanent, failindex.SourceWorkerPermanent, err)
     116			slog.WarnContext(ctx, "Permanent sync error, terminating", slog.Any("error", err))
     117			c.metrics.recordSyncOutcome(ctx, provider, c.jurisdiction, outcomeTerm)
     118			_ = m.Term()

     122				// so this is terminal. Best-effort record (max_deliveries), then Term —
     123				// fail open (recordTerminal surfaces a write failure via record_failed).
     124				c.recordTerminal(ctx, ulid, subject, payload, seq, failindex.ReasonMaxDeliveries, failindex.SourceWorkerExhausted, err)
     125				slog.ErrorContext(ctx, "Sync failed; deliveries exhausted, terminating", slog.Any("error", err))
     126				c.metrics.recordSyncOutcome(ctx, provider, c.jurisdiction, outcomeTerm)
     127				_ = m.Term()
     128				return
     129			}
     130			slog.ErrorContext(ctx, "Sync failed", slog.Any("error", err))
     131			c.metrics.recordSyncOutcome(ctx, provider, c.jurisdiction, outcomeNak)
     132			_ = m.NakWithDelay(consumerFailureDelay)

     134			// Target ref moved between this job's plan and its push — a second sync
     135			// of the same repo won the race (rapid webhooks, or a webhook
     136			// overlapping the rekicker). Redeliver like a nak so the next delivery
     137			// re-plans against the new tip and applies cleanly (usually a no-op,
     138			// since the winner already mirrored the same source state), but record
     139			// outcomeRaced + WARN so it stays out of the sync-failure alert's
     140			// nak+term numerator: it self-heals and pages no one.
     141			//
     142			// Final-delivery escalation is identical to the nak path: if a repo
     143			// somehow races every delivery to exhaustion the contention is no longer
     144			// benign, so we record terminal (max_deliveries) and label outcome=term
     145			// — that DOES reach the alert, which is what we want for a genuinely
     146			// stuck repo. Fail open on the index write exactly like dispositionNak.
     147			if isLastDelivery(m) {
     148				c.recordTerminal(ctx, ulid, subject, payload, seq, failindex.ReasonMaxDeliveries, failindex.SourceWorkerExhausted, err)
     149				slog.ErrorContext(ctx, "Sync kept racing on target ref; deliveries exhausted, terminating", slog.Any("error", err))
     150				c.metrics.recordSyncOutcome(ctx, provider, c.jurisdiction, outcomeTerm)
     151				_ = m.Term()

     153			}
     154			slog.WarnContext(ctx, "Sync raced on target ref; will retry", slog.Any("error", err))

     157		}
     158	}

     160	// disposition is the JetStream action disposeResult takes for a sync result.
     161	type disposition int
     162	
     163	const (
     164		dispositionAck   disposition = iota // success → Ack
     165		dispositionNoop                     // benign empty-source no-op → Ack
     166		dispositionTerm                     // permanent failure → Term (no redelivery)
     167		dispositionNak                      // transient failure → Nak (redeliver)

     184			// concurrent-ref phrase still terminates; concurrent-ref errors are
     185			// never marked permanent, so this only ever reclassifies the transient
     186			// (would-be-nak) tail.
     187			return dispositionRaced
     188		default:
     189			return dispositionNak
     190		}

Confirmed: no in-repo monitor computes the failure rate (the `publisher.go` hit is an unrelated webhook metric), the in-process retry loop doesn't retry races (they flow straight to JetStream redelivery), and both `isLastDelivery` call sites read the same message metadata. the new tip, usually a no-op). The PR introduces a new disposition "raced":
- isConcurrentRefError(err) = errors.Is(err, gitsync.ErrTargetRefMoved)  [cmd/worker/syncer_errors.go]
- classifyDisposition routes raced AFTER isPermanentError [cmd/worker/consumer.go]
- disposeResult: raced -> NakWithDelay + outcome:raced + WARN (self-heals); on final delivery
  (NumDelivered >= consumerMaxDeliver=10) escalates to Term + max_deliveries + outcome:term.
- shouldMarkSpanError(err, lastDelivery) [syncer_errors.go]: keeps a redelivering race off the
  mirror.consume APM error span; an exhausted race (lastDelivery) stays marked.
- span tagged entire.sync.raced=true whenever isConcurrentRefError.
- git-sync vendored bump v0.6.0 -> pseudo-version; ErrTargetRefMoved is now a typed sentinel
  (vendor/entire.io/entire/git-sync/errors.go + internal/gitproto/push.go).
- go.mod also moves github.com/nats-io/nuid from indirect to direct (used in cmd/rekicker/rekick.go).

GROUND TRUTH already established by the lead reviewer (you may rely on these, but re-verify if relevant):
- go build ./..., go vet ./cmd/worker/, gofmt -l, and go test ./cmd/worker/ ALL PASS in this env (go 1.26).
- No in-repo dashboard/monitor/terraform computes the failure rate; the only other "outcome" metric is
  an unrelated webhook publisher metric in pkg/webhookevents/publisher.go.
- The in-process retry loop (syncer.go, maxSyncAttempts=3) only retries isTransientGitFetchError (http 404
  fetch), NOT push/concurrent-ref errors — so a race breaks the loop on attempt 1 and flows to
  classifySyncError(redactAuth(lastErr)). Redelivery happens at the JetStream layer.
- redactAuth (syncer_errors.go) returns the ORIGINAL error (chain intact) UNLESS the auth regex matches,
  in which case it flattens to errors.New(redacted) — which would drop the ErrTargetRefMoved sentinel.
- Both isLastDelivery call sites (handleGitJob span-marking + disposeResult) read the same *nats.Msg
  metadata within one handleGitJob call.

Be a skeptical, independent reviewer. Findings must be concrete (file:line), with a clear severity
(blocker / high / medium / low / nit / note) and an honest take on whether it is a REAL problem in
production or only a contrived/theoretical one. Do NOT invent issues to seem thorough; "no issues in
this dimension" is a valid and valuable result. Prefer correctness and real-world impact over style.
`

const FINDINGS_SCHEMA = {
  type: 'object',
  additionalProperties: false,
  required: ['dimension', 'findings'],
  properties: {
    dimension: { type: 'string' },
    findings: {
      type: 'array',
      items: {
        type: 'object',
        additionalProperties: false,
        required: ['title', 'severity', 'location', 'claim', 'reasoning', 'realWorldImpact', 'suggestedFix'],
        properties: {
          title: { type: 'string' },
          severity: { type: 'string', enum: ['blocker', 'high', 'medium', 'low', 'nit', 'note'] },
          location: { type: 'string', description: 'file:line(s)' },
          claim: { type: 'string', description: 'one-sentence statement of the issue' },
          reasoning: { type: 'string' },
          realWorldImpact: { type: 'string', description: 'realistic vs contrived; what actually breaks in prod' },
          suggestedFix: { type: 'string' },
        },
      },
    },
  },
}

const VERDICT_SCHEMA = {
  type: 'object',
  additionalProperties: false,
  required: ['verdict', 'confidence', 'rebuttal', 'correctedSeverity'],
  properties: {
    verdict: { type: 'string', enum: ['confirmed', 'refuted', 'partial'] },
    confidence: { type: 'string', enum: ['high', 'medium', 'low'] },
    rebuttal: { type: 'string', description: 'the strongest case that this finding is wrong or overstated, and whether it survives' },
    correctedSeverity: { type: 'string', enum: ['blocker', 'high', 'medium', 'low', 'nit', 'note', 'invalid'] },
  },
}

const DIMENSIONS = [
  {
    key: 'error-classification',
    prompt: `${CONTEXT}

DIMENSION: Error classification correctness.
Scrutinize the error path end to end:
- Does errors.Is(err, gitsync.ErrTargetRefMoved) actually survive the worker's pipeline:
  git-sync wrapped error -> redactAuth -> classifySyncError(syncError wrapper, Unwrap) -> classifyDisposition?
  Verify the *RefRejectedError.Is/Unwrap and syncError.Unwrap chains in the vendored code and syncer_errors.go.
- classifyDisposition ordering: isPermanentError before isConcurrentRefError. Can a real production error be
  BOTH permanent and ErrTargetRefMoved? Trace classifySyncError's permanentStatusHints vs the actual receive-pack
  reason text. Is the "permanent beats raced" defense necessary or dead?
- The redactAuth flattening edge: can a real concurrent-ref error message ever match authHeaderPattern
  (Basic/Bearer + token chars)? If so the sentinel is lost and it degrades to nak. Real or impossible?
- Any case where a GENUINE failure (e.g. source rewind needing force, a real non-fast-forward) is now
  misclassified as a benign race and silently swallowed? Check git-sync's concurrentMoveMarkers
  (push.go) — is "remote ref has changed"/"stale info" too broad? Could a non-race rejection set moved=true?
Read: cmd/worker/syncer_errors.go, cmd/worker/consumer.go, cmd/worker/syncer.go,
vendor/entire.io/entire/git-sync/errors.go, vendor/entire.io/entire/git-sync/internal/gitproto/push.go.`,
  },
  {
    key: 'observability-consistency',
    prompt: `${CONTEXT}

DIMENSION: Observability / metrics / span consistency.
- shouldMarkSpanError vs classifyDisposition: they are meant to "mirror each other's notion of failure."
  Do they actually agree in EVERY case? Build the full truth table over {nil, errSourceNoRefs, plain transient,
  permanent, raced-nonfinal, raced-final, permanent-AND-raced} x {lastDelivery true/false}. Note that
  shouldMarkSpanError does NOT check isPermanentError but classifyDisposition does. Where do they diverge, and
  is that divergence reachable in prod or only contrived?
- entire.sync.raced tag is set whenever isConcurrentRefError regardless of lastDelivery — so an exhausted race
  gets BOTH raced=true AND span error + outcome=term. Intended? Confusing in traces?
- last_success_unix: confirm outcome:raced (and term/nak/noop) do NOT stamp it, only ack. Check metrics.go.
- Metric description string updated consistently; the monitor numerator names only nak+term so raced drops out
  of the alert but stays in the denominator (diluting the rate). Is diluting the rate the right call, or could
  it mask a rising real-failure rate on a repo that also races a lot? Reason about the math.
- Log levels: raced non-final -> WARN, raced exhausted -> ERROR, term -> WARN (existing), nak -> ERROR. Is the
  WARN-for-raced / ERROR-for-permanent-term split sensible and consistent?
Read: cmd/worker/consumer.go, cmd/worker/metrics.go, cmd/worker/syncer_errors.go.`,
  },
  {
    key: 'redelivery-semantics',
    prompt: `${CONTEXT}

DIMENSION: Concurrency, redelivery & idempotency semantics.
- raced -> NakWithDelay(consumerFailureDelay=30s). On a busy repo racing repeatedly, this consumes delivery
  attempts toward consumerMaxDeliver=10. Could a legitimately-busy repo (frequent pushes) exhaust deliveries
  and ESCALATE TO TERM (false max_deliveries) even though each individual race is benign? Reason about the
  realistic webhook/rekicker cadence (15-min rekicker, rapid webhooks) vs 10 deliveries x 30s = 5 min window.
  Is this a real risk of false termination? This is the most important question in this dimension.
- Is the re-plan-on-redelivery actually idempotent/safe in ALL race scenarios (winner mirrored same state ->
  no-op; source advanced -> applies newer)? Any scenario where redelivery does the WRONG thing?
- Final-delivery raced -> recordTerminal(max_deliveries) + Term. Does this correctly fail-open on index write
  error like the nak path? Compare the two branches in disposeResult.
- Does counting outcome:raced on EVERY redelivery (not just once) distort anything? e.g. one logical race
  producing N raced ticks before self-healing.
Read: cmd/worker/consumer.go (disposeResult, isLastDelivery, recordTerminal), cmd/worker/syncer.go.`,
  },
  {
    key: 'tests',
    prompt: `${CONTEXT}

DIMENSION: Test coverage and test correctness.
- Read cmd/worker/syncer_errors_test.go and cmd/worker/consumer_failindex_test.go (the new tests).
- Do the tests actually assert what their names/comments claim? Any test that passes trivially / asserts the
  wrong thing / would still pass if the feature were broken?
- Coverage gaps: is there a test for the shouldMarkSpanError permanent-AND-raced case? For the redactAuth
  flattening edge? For the busy-repo-exhaustion-to-term path at the consumer level? For entire.sync.raced tagging
  in handleGitJob? Are the missing ones worth adding, or contrived?
- The tests construct ErrTargetRefMoved by wrapping the sentinel directly (push.go says the concrete moved=true
  RefRejectedError "cannot be reproduced by external construction"). Does that make the tests UNFAITHFUL to how
  the error arrives in prod (where it's a *RefRejectedError, not a bare wrap)? Does errors.Is behave the same for
  both? Verify.
- Is TestConcurrentMove_SurvivesWorkerClassification a faithful end-to-end (redactAuth+classifySyncError+
  classifyDisposition)? What does it NOT cover that prod hits?
Read the two *_test.go files and the helpers (msgAt, newConsumer, newTestMetrics, counterValue, fakeIndex).`,
  },
  {
    key: 'deps-build-hygiene',
    prompt: `${CONTEXT}

DIMENSION: Vendored dependency, go.mod/go.sum, and build hygiene.
- go.mod pins git-sync to a PSEUDO-VERSION (v0.6.1-0.20260613011240-92066eebf9d8), a main-branch commit, with a
  PR note to repin to v0.7.0 once released. Risks of merging a pseudo-version dep? Is the vendored tree
  (vendor/modules.txt, vendor/.../git-sync/*) consistent with go.mod/go.sum? Run: cd ${WT} && go mod verify;
  go build ./...; and check git diff origin/main...HEAD -- go.sum vendor/modules.txt for coherence.
- nuid moved from indirect to direct: confirm cmd/rekicker/rekick.go imports it directly and that this is a
  correct (if unrelated-to-PR-scope) go mod tidy outcome, not an accidental dependency change. Is bundling it
  here acceptable?
- Does the vendored git-sync diff include ONLY what's needed (errors.go, push.go, syncer.go), or did unrelated
  library changes ride along? Skim vendor/.../git-sync/CHANGELOG.md and the syncer.go diff for scope creep or
  behavioral changes in git-sync that the worker now depends on implicitly.
- Any go.sum entries missing/stale that would break a clean 'go build' for someone without the module cache?
Read/run against ${WT}.`,
  },
]

phase('Review')
log(`Reviewing PR #135 across ${DIMENSIONS.length} dimensions, verifying each finding adversarially`)

const reviewed = await pipeline(
  DIMENSIONS,
  d => agent(d.prompt, { label: `review:${d.key}`, phase: 'Review', schema: FINDINGS_SCHEMA }),
  (review, d) => {
    const findings = (review && review.findings) || []
    // Only adversarially verify findings that claim a real defect (skip pure 'note' FYIs to save effort,
    // but keep low+ so we don't drop a real-but-small issue).
    const toVerify = findings.filter(f => f.severity !== 'note')
    if (toVerify.length === 0) return { dimension: d.key, verified: [], notes: findings }
    return parallel(toVerify.map(f => () =>
      agent(`${CONTEXT}

ADVERSARIALLY VERIFY this finding from the '${d.key}' review. Your job is to REFUTE it if you can —
read the actual code at ${WT} and construct the strongest argument that the finding is wrong, overstated,
or only contrived. Then deliver a verdict. Default to skepticism: if the "problem" only manifests in a
contrived/unreachable scenario, say so and downgrade. If it's real, confirm it and keep/raise severity.

FINDING:
  title: ${f.title}
  severity(claimed): ${f.severity}
  location: ${f.location}
  claim: ${f.claim}
  reasoning: ${f.reasoning}
  realWorldImpact(claimed): ${f.realWorldImpact}
  suggestedFix: ${f.suggestedFix}`,
        { label: `verify:${d.key}:${f.title.slice(0, 28)}`, phase: 'Verify', schema: VERDICT_SCHEMA })
        .then(v => ({ ...f, dimension: d.key, verdict: v }))
    )).then(verified => ({ dimension: d.key, verified, notes: findings.filter(f => f.severity === 'note') }))
  }
)

// Flatten verified findings + notes across dimensions
const allVerified = reviewed.flatMap(r => (r && r.verified) || [])
const allNotes = reviewed.flatMap(r => (r && r.notes) || [])
const surviving = allVerified.filter(f => f.verdict && f.verdict.verdict !== 'refuted' && f.verdict.correctedSeverity !== 'invalid')
const refuted = allVerified.filter(f => f.verdict && (f.verdict.verdict === 'refuted' || f.verdict.correctedSeverity === 'invalid'))

phase('Synthesize')
log(`Findings: ${surviving.length} survived verification, ${refuted.length} refuted, ${allNotes.length} notes`)

const synthesis = await agent(`${CONTEXT}

You are writing the final review verdict for PR #135. Below are the findings that SURVIVED adversarial
verification, the REFUTED ones (mention briefly so the reader knows they were considered and dismissed),
and lower-priority NOTES. Synthesize a single, well-structured PR review in markdown.

Requirements:
- Lead with a one-line overall verdict (Approve / Approve with nits / Request changes) and a 2-3 sentence summary
  of what the PR does and whether it's sound.
- Group surviving findings by severity, highest first. For each: the issue, file:line, why it matters in prod
  (be honest about contrived vs real), and a concrete fix. Merge duplicates that multiple dimensions found.
- A short "Considered and dismissed" section listing refuted findings in one line each.
- A "Strengths" section (this is a careful PR — call out what's done well: ordering defense, fail-open parity,
  test rigor, typed-sentinel migration, monitor math).
- Be precise and concise. No filler. Use the reviewer's own voice. Do NOT pad severity — if the right answer is
  "approve, the two nits are optional," say exactly that.

SURVIVING FINDINGS (post-verification):
${JSON.stringify(surviving.map(f => ({ title: f.title, severity: f.severity, correctedSeverity: f.verdict.correctedSeverity, location: f.location, claim: f.claim, reasoning: f.reasoning, realWorldImpact: f.realWorldImpact, suggestedFix: f.suggestedFix, verifierRebuttal: f.verdict.rebuttal, confidence: f.verdict.confidence })), null, 2)}

REFUTED / DISMISSED:
${JSON.stringify(refuted.map(f => ({ title: f.title, location: f.location, claim: f.claim, whyDismissed: f.verdict.rebuttal })), null, 2)}

NOTES (low-priority FYIs):
${JSON.stringify(allNotes.map(f => ({ title: f.title, location: f.location, claim: f.claim })), null, 2)}
`, { label: 'synthesize', phase: 'Synthesize' })

return { synthesis, counts: { surviving: surviving.length, refuted: refuted.length, notes: allNotes.length } }

content.text:
I've completed the scouting pass and launched a comprehensive review workflow. Here's where things stand while it runs.

**What I've verified independently so far:**
- ✅ `go build ./...`, `go vet ./cmd/worker/`, `gofmt -l`, and `go test ./cmd/worker/` all pass (go 1.26) — the "all green" claim holds.
- ✅ The `errors.Is(err, gitsync.ErrTargetRefMoved)` chain survives the real pipeline (`*RefRejectedError.Is`/`Unwrap` → `syncError.Unwrap`), and the in-process retry loop doesn't retry races — they flow straight to JetStream redelivery as intended.
- ✅ No in-repo monitor/dashboard computes the failure rate, so `raced` correctly drops out of the external `nak+term` numerator (the `publisher.go` hit is an unrelated webhook metric).
- ✅ `nuid` moving to a direct dep is a legitimate `go mod tidy` correction (`cmd/rekicker/rekick.go` imports it).

**Two candidate findings I want the workflow to adversarially confirm or kill:**
1. `shouldMarkSpanError` doesn't check `isPermanentError` (unlike `classifyDisposition`), so a *permanent-AND-raced* error would be `Term`'d but left unmarked on the span — likely contrived, but the disposition layer defends against exactly this case and the span layer doesn't.
2. `redactAuth` flattens the error to `errors.New` when the auth regex matches, which would drop the typed sentinel and degrade a race back to `nak` — only matters if a race message ever carries a `Basic/Bearer` token.

The workflow is fanning out across 5 dimensions (error classification, observability consistency, redelivery semantics, tests, dep/build hygiene), adversarially verifying each finding, then synthesizing. It's running in the background against the PR-head worktree at `/tmp/pr135-wt`. I'll post the full review when it completes.

# PR #135 Review — `worker/raced-outcome-concurrent-ref`

## Verdict: Request changes

This PR introduces a `raced` disposition so that entire-server compare-and-swap misses ("remote ref has changed") stop being counted as `nak` failures that feed the sync-failure-rate monitor. The classification logic, the Nak-then-Term escalation, the span/error-marking carve-out, and the test scaffolding around the *worker* are well-designed and sound. **But the typed sentinel that all of this hinges on is never actually attached on the real receive-pack path**, so on the production path the PR changes nothing observable — every real race still classifies `nak`. That one bug is a blocker; everything else is low/nit/note.

---

## Blocker

### `ErrTargetRefMoved` is never attached on the real push path — the feature is dead code in production
`vendor/entire.io/entire/git-sync/internal/gitproto/push.go:238-249` (`asRefRejectedError`), consumed at `push.go:302`.

`asRefRejectedError` does `errors.As(err, &cs)` with `var cs *packp.CommandStatusErr`, but go-git v6 returns `CommandStatusErr` **by value** (value receiver `Error()`, constructed by value at go-git `report_status.go:185-188`). `errors.As` against a `**CommandStatusErr` target never matches a value-typed `CommandStatusErr`, so the `*RefRejectedError` is never built, `errors.Is(err, ErrTargetRefMoved)` stays `false`, and a real CAS miss flows to `dispositionNak` exactly as it did before this PR.

This was proven three ways: (1) `report.Error()`'s dynamic type is the value `packp.CommandStatusErr`; (2) driving a real `Encode -> Decode -> report.Error()` for `ng refs/heads/main remote ref has changed` through the exact `sendReceivePack` line yields `errors.Is(ErrTargetRefMoved) == false`; (3) switching the target to `var cs packp.CommandStatusErr` (value) makes it `true`. The worker uses `Replicate` without `BestEffort`, so `OnRejection` is nil (`syncer.go:726`) and `sendReceivePack` takes the `onRejection == nil` branch (`push.go:300-304`) — the only place the sentinel is ever attached. All three push helpers funnel through that one line; there is no alternate path.

**Why it matters in prod (real, not contrived):** this is the entire point of the PR. Every entire-server CAS miss still logs ERROR "Sync failed", counts `outcome:nak`, and feeds the monitor's `nak+term` numerator. The new `raced` disposition, the `outcome:raced` metric, the `entire.sync.raced` span tag, and the `shouldMarkSpanError` carve-out are all dead on the real path. CI is green only because every worker test fabricates the error via `fmt.Errorf("...: %w", gitsync.ErrTargetRefMoved)` (`syncer_errors_test.go:73,98-99,124,137`) rather than exercising git-sync's real push — the `push.go` doc comment even instructs downstream tests to wrap the sentinel directly "because the deciding field is unexported." The same broken pointer-target pattern is in the pre-existing `annotateLeaseFailure` (`push.go:133`), so the lease-hint annotation has silently never fired either.

**Fix:**
1. In `asRefRejectedError` (and `annotateLeaseFailure`) change the target to a value: `var cs packp.CommandStatusErr; if !errors.As(err, &cs) { return err }`.
2. Add a git-sync-side test that drives a real `Encode -> Decode -> report.Error()` (or `report.Error()` directly) through `asRefRejectedError` and asserts `errors.Is(ErrTargetRefMoved)`.
3. In the worker, add one test that does **not** pre-wrap the sentinel — feed a value `packp.CommandStatusErr{Status:"remote ref has changed"}` through the same chain — so a regression of this exact bug is caught instead of being papered over.

This finding must be fixed before merge; without it, the rest of the PR is inert.

---

## Low

### False TERM on a busy repo — real but narrow
`cmd/worker/consumer.go:362-385` (`dispositionRaced`), `:442-447` (`isLastDelivery`), `:21-22` (`consumerFailureDelay=30s`, `consumerMaxDeliver=10`).

The Term escalation keys on **one** stream message's `NumDelivered >= 10`. Because mirror jobs are content-addressed by `Nats-Msg-Id` with a 1s dedup window on a WorkQueue stream, rapid pushes spawn *distinct* messages (each `NumDelivered=1`), not extra deliveries of one message. For a given message to escalate it must personally lose the CAS on 9 consecutive 30s-spaced redeliveries (>4.5 min), while on every redelivery re-planning against the live tip would normally make it a clean no-op `Ack`. So this only fires under sustained, minutes-long multi-replica contention on the *same* message — and even then the rekicker re-emits with a fresh `Msg-Id` (resetting `NumDelivered`) and self-heals within ~15 min.

**Why it matters:** the path is reachable and a false `outcome:term` does reach the monitor numerator, so it can page. Just rarely, and not from ordinary "frequent pushes."

**Fix (optional, no correctness change):** consider a distinct `outcome=raced_exhausted` label instead of folding into `outcome=term` so the monitor can tell a race-storm from a genuinely-stuck repo, or a higher `MaxDeliver`/jittered nak delay for the raced disposition. Document the tradeoff in the `disposeResult` comment.

### `redactAuth` ordering can drop the sentinel if a move reason ever embeds an auth-shaped token
`cmd/worker/syncer.go:288` (`classifySyncError(redactAuth(lastErr))`); `cmd/worker/syncer_errors.go:124-133` (`redactAuth`), `:86-88` (`isConcurrentRefError`).

`redactAuth` runs *before* classification and, on an `authHeaderPattern` match, flattens to `errors.New(redacted)` — which has no `Unwrap`, severing the `ErrTargetRefMoved` sentinel and reclassifying `raced -> nak` (and re-marking the span error). I confirmed the mechanism empirically. It is **not reachable with the entire-server target**: the sentinel rides a 200-OK report-status `CommandStatusErr` ("remote ref has changed", token-free), while `authHeaderPattern` defends a different path (the smarthttp transport echoing up to 64KB of a non-2xx response body) that never carries the sentinel. The two live on architecturally disjoint HTTP paths.

**Why it matters:** contrived for today's target, but the sentinel-survival contract silently depends on `redactAuth` not matching, and that dependency is undocumented and untested. (Also moot until the blocker is fixed.)

**Fix:** classify the sentinel on the *raw* `lastErr` (thread a `raced`/`permanent` bool out of `sync`) and let `redactAuth` only sanitize the display string, never change disposition. At minimum, add a regression test pinning `errors.Is(redactAuth(x), ErrTargetRefMoved)` when the message also contains a `Bearer`-like token.

### `shouldMarkSpanError` breaks its "mirrors the disposition layer" invariant for permanent-AND-raced
`cmd/worker/syncer_errors.go:99-110` vs `cmd/worker/consumer.go:403-420`; missing test at `syncer_errors_test.go:135-159`.

`shouldMarkSpanError` checks `isConcurrentRefError(err) && !lastDelivery` and returns `false` *without first excluding permanent errors*, whereas `classifyDisposition` checks `isPermanentError` **before** `isConcurrentRefError`. So for an error that is both permanent and sentinel-bearing on a non-final delivery, `classifyDisposition` returns `dispositionTerm` while `shouldMarkSpanError` returns `false` — the span stays unmarked. Reproduced in-env.

**Why it matters:** contrived — `classifySyncError` cannot currently produce an error that is both permanent and sentinel-bearing (different protocol phases), so the only consequence even if forced is one under-marked APM span; the Term disposition and `outcome:term` stay correct. But the doc comment explicitly promises the two layers agree, and they don't.

**Fix:** reorder `shouldMarkSpanError` to short-circuit `isPermanentError(err)` before the `isConcur

### `ErrTargetRefMoved` is never attached on the real push path — the feature is dead code in production
`vendor/entire.io/entire/git-sync/internal/gitproto/push.go:238-249` (`asRefRejectedError`), consumed at `push.go:302`.

`asRefRejectedError` does `errors.As(err, &cs)` with `var cs *packp.CommandStatusErr`, but go-git v6 returns `CommandStatusErr` **by value** (value receiver `Error()`, constructed by value at go-git `report_status.go:185-188`). `errors.As` against a `**CommandStatusErr` target never matches a value-typed `CommandStatusErr`, so the `*RefRejectedError` is never built, `errors.Is(err, ErrTargetRefMoved)` stays `false`, and a real CAS miss flows to `dispositionNak` exactly as it did before this PR.

This was proven three ways: (1) `report.Error()`'s dynamic type is the value `packp.CommandStatusErr`; (2) driving a real `Encode -> Decode -> report.Error()` for `ng refs/heads/main remote ref has changed` through the exact `sendReceivePack` line yields `errors.Is(ErrTargetRefMoved) == false`; (3) switching the target to `var cs packp.CommandStatusErr` (value) makes it `true`. The worker uses `Replicate` without `BestEffort`, so `OnRejection` is nil (`syncer.go:726`) and `sendReceivePack` takes the `onRejection == nil` branch (`push.go:300-304`) — the only place the sentinel is ever attached. All three push helpers funnel through that one line; there is no alternate path.

**Why it matters in prod (real, not contrived):** this is the entire point of the PR. Every entire-server CAS miss still logs ERROR "Sync failed", counts `outcome:nak`, and feeds the monitor's `nak+term` numerator. The new `raced` disposition, the `outcome:raced` metric, the `entire.sync.raced` span tag, and the `shouldMarkSpanError` carve-out are all dead on the real path. CI is green only because every worker test fabricates the error via `fmt.Errorf("...: %w", gitsync.ErrTargetRefMoved)` (`syncer_errors_test.go:73,98-99,124,137`) rather than exercising git-sync's real push — the `push.go` doc comment even instructs downstream tests to wrap the sentinel directly "because the deciding field is unexported." The same broken pointer-target pattern is in the pre-existing `annotateLeaseFailure` (`push.go:133`), so the lease-hint annotation has silently never fired either.

**Fix:**
1. In `asRefRejectedError` (and `annotateLeaseFailure`) change the target to a value: `var cs packp.CommandStatusErr; if !errors.As(err, &cs) { return err }`.
2. Add a git-sync-side test that drives a real `Encode -> Decode -> report.Error()` (or `report.Error()` directly) through `asRefRejectedError` and asserts `errors.Is(ErrTargetRefMoved)`.
3. In the worker, add one test that does **not** pre-wrap the sentinel — feed a value `packp.CommandStatusErr{Status:"remote ref has changed"}` through the same chain — so a regression of this exact bug is caught instead of being papered over.

This finding must be fixed before merge; without it, the rest of the PR is inert.

---

## Low

### False TERM on a busy repo — real but narrow
`cmd/worker/consumer.go:362-385` (`dispositionRaced`), `:442-447` (`isLastDelivery`), `:21-22` (`consumerFailureDelay=30s`, `consumerMaxDeliver=10`).

The Term escalation keys on **one** stream message's `NumDelivered >= 10`. Because mirror jobs are content-addressed by `Nats-Msg-Id` with a 1s dedup window on a WorkQueue stream, rapid pushes spawn *distinct* messages (each `NumDelivered=1`), not extra deliveries of one message. For a given message to escalate it must personally lose the CAS on 9 consecutive 30s-spaced redeliveries (>4.5 min), while on every redelivery re-planning against the live tip would normally make it a clean no-op `Ack`. So this only fires under sustained, minutes-long multi-replica contention on the *same* message — and even then the rekicker re-emits with a fresh `Msg-Id` (resetting `NumDelivered`) and self-heals within ~15 min.

**Why it matters:** the path is reachable and a false `outcome:term` does reach the monitor numerator, so it can page. Just rarely, and not from ordinary "frequent pushes."

**Fix (optional, no correctness change):** consider a distinct `outcome=raced_exhausted` label instead of folding into `outcome=term` so the monitor can tell a race-storm from a genuinely-stuck repo, or a higher `MaxDeliver`/jittered nak delay for the raced disposition. Document the tradeoff in the `disposeResult` comment.

### `redactAuth` ordering can drop the sentinel if a move reason ever embeds an auth-shaped token
`cmd/worker/syncer.go:288` (`classifySyncError(redactAuth(lastErr))`); `cmd/worker/syncer_errors.go:124-133` (`redactAuth`), `:86-88` (`isConcurrentRefError`).

`redactAuth` runs *before* classification and, on an `authHeaderPattern` match, flattens to `errors.New(redacted)` — which has no `Unwrap`, severing the `ErrTargetRefMoved` sentinel and reclassifying `raced -> nak` (and re-marking the span error). I confirmed the mechanism empirically. It is **not reachable with the entire-server target**: the sentinel rides a 200-OK report-status `CommandStatusErr` ("remote ref has changed", token-free), while `authHeaderPattern` defends a different path (the smarthttp transport echoing up to 64KB of a non-2xx response body) that never carries the sentinel. The two live on architecturally disjoint HTTP paths.

**Why it matters:** contrived for today's target, but the sentinel-survival contract silently depends on `redactAuth` not matching, and that dependency is undocumented and untested. (Also moot until the blocker is fixed.)

**Fix:** classify the sentinel on the *raw* `lastErr` (thread a `raced`/`permanent` bool out of `sync`) and let `redactAuth` only sanitize the display string, never change disposition. At minimum, add a regression test pinning `errors.Is(redactAuth(x), ErrTargetRefMoved)` when the message also contains a `Bearer`-like token.

### `shouldMarkSpanError` breaks its "mirrors the disposition layer" invariant for permanent-AND-raced
`cmd/worker/syncer_errors.go:99-110` vs `cmd/worker/consumer.go:403-420`; missing test at `syncer_errors_test.go:135-159`.

`shouldMarkSpanError` checks `isConcurrentRefError(err) && !lastDelivery` and returns `false` *without first excluding permanent errors*, whereas `classifyDisposition` checks `isPermanentError` **before** `isConcurrentRefError`. So for an error that is both permanent and sentinel-bearing on a non-final delivery, `classifyDisposition` returns `dispositionTerm` while `shouldMarkSpanError` returns `false` — the span stays unmarked. Reproduced in-env.

**Why it matters:** contrived — `classifySyncError` cannot currently produce an error that is both permanent and sentinel-bearing (different protocol phases), so the only consequence even if forced is one under-marked APM span; the Term disposition and `outcome:term` stay correct. But the doc comment explicitly promises the two layers agree, and they don't.

**Fix:** reorder `shouldMarkSpanError` to short-circuit `isPermanentError(err)` before the `isConcur

content.text:
The workflow finished and surfaced a **BLOCKER** that survived adversarial verification with high confidence: the typed `ErrTargetRefMoved` sentinel is allegedly never attached on the real push path, because `asRefRejectedError` does `errors.As(err, &cs)` with `var cs *packp.CommandStatusErr`, but go-git v6 supposedly returns `CommandStatusErr` **by value**.

This is a strong claim — it says the whole feature is inert in production. I will **not** relay it without independently verifying the crux myself: does go-git v6's `report.Error()` return a value or a pointer? Let me check the vendored go-git source directly, and read the full synthesis.

input.command: cd /tmp/pr135-wt && echo "=== locate CommandStatusErr in vendored go-git v6 ==="; grep -rn "CommandStatusErr" vendor/github.com/go-git/go-git/v6/plumbing/protocol/packp/report_status.go 2>/dev/null; echo "=== the Error() method + how it's returned ==="; grep -rn "CommandStatusErr\b" vendor/github.com/go-git/go-git/v6/plumbing/protocol/packp/*.go
input.description: Find CommandStatusErr definition and return in go-git

content.text: Confirmed the smoking gun at the source: line 35 `func (e CommandStatusErr) Error() string` (value receiver) and line 185 `return CommandStatusErr{` (constructed by value). Let me read the exact construction and then prove the `errors.As` mismatch empirically with a focused reproduction test rather than trusting the reasoning.

input.command: cd /tmp/pr135-wt && sed -n '26,50p' vendor/github.com/go-git/go-git/v6/plumbing/protocol/packp/report_status.go; echo "..."; sed -n '160,195p' vendor/github.com/go-git/go-git/v6/plumbing/protocol/packp/report_status.go
input.description: Read CommandStatusErr definition and Error() construction

content.text:
Source confirms it analytically: `CommandStatus.Error()` returns `CommandStatusErr{...}` **by value** (line ~185) with a **value receiver** `Error()` (line 35). So `errors.As(err, &cs)` with `var cs *packp.CommandStatusErr` (a `**CommandStatusErr` target) can't match a value-typed `CommandStatusErr` in the chain.

Let me prove this empirically rather than trust the reasoning — a throwaway test driving the exact error go-git hands to git-sync:

content.text:
**Empirically confirmed.** The throwaway test driving the exact error go-git hands git-sync proves it decisively:

- `report.Error()` dynamic type = `packp.CommandStatusErr` (**value**, not pointer)
- `errors.As(err, **CommandStatusErr)` = **false** ← git-sync's actual target in `asRefRejectedError`/`annotateLeaseFailure`
- `errors.As(err, *CommandStatusErr)` = **true** ← the fix
- `errors.Is(wrapped, gitsync.ErrTargetRefMoved)` = **false** on the real path

So in production, every real CAS miss yields a value-typed `CommandStatusErr`, git-sync's pointer-target `errors.As` misses, no `*RefRejectedError` is built, `moved` is never set, and `isConcurrentRefError` always returns `false`. The feature is inert. Let me confirm there's no *other* path in vendored git-sync that attaches the sentinel, so I don't overstate the blocker.

input.command: cd /tmp/pr135-wt && echo "=== every reference to ErrTargetRefMoved / moved / asRefRejectedError in vendored git-sync ==="; grep -rn "ErrTargetRefMoved\|asRefRejectedError\|\.moved\|moved:\|moved \|OnRejection\|BestEffort" vendor/entire.io/entire/git-sync/ | grep -v "_test.go"; echo; echo "=== does the worker ever set BestEffort / OnRejection? ==="; grep -rn "BestEffort\|OnRejection" cmd/worker/ ; echo "(none above = worker uses default strict push path)"
input.description: Confirm sole sentinel-attachment path in git-sync

vendor/entire.io/entire/git-sync/CHANGELOG.md:12:- Typed push-rejection errors on the public API. `Sync`/`Replicate` now report a `*RefRejectedError` (carrying the rejected `Ref` and the raw server `Reason`) for per-ref receive-pack `ng` statuses, reachable with `errors.As`. Rejections that are unambiguous concurrent target-ref moves — entire-server's compare-and-swap rejection (`remote ref has changed`) and git's `--force-with-lease` lease miss (`stale info`) — additionally satisfy `errors.Is(err, ErrTargetRefMoved)`. This lets embedders distinguish a benign racing concurrent push (retryable) from a genuine push failure without substring-matching the free-form error message. Ambiguous markers (`non-fast-forward` / `fetch first`) are deliberately excluded from the move classification so a real "needs `--force`" rejection is not masked. The `ForceWithLease` lease-failure escalation (raised even under `BestEffort`) also satisfies `errors.Is(err, ErrTargetRefMoved)`, though it is not itself a `*RefRejectedError` — prefer `errors.Is` over `errors.As` when you only need the cause. The worker uses neither `BestEffort` nor `OnRejection` nor force flags (grep returned nothing in `cmd/worker/`), so `asRefRejectedError` at `push.go:302` is the **sole** sentinel-attachment path for the worker — and it's the broken one. The only other wrap (`syncer.go:475`) requires `BestEffort + ForceWithLease`, which the worker never sets.
